entries.title: Tiger Woods says he'll step away from golf and seek treatment after accident
entries.summary: <p>ORLANDO, Fla. — Tiger Woods said he'll step away and seek treatment on Tuesday, days after his vehicle crashed in Florida and he was arrested on suspicion of driving under the influence.</p><p>"This is necessary in order for me to prioritize my well-being and work toward lasting recovery," he said in a post on the social media platform X.</p><p>Woods pleaded not guilty in his driving under the influence case in Florida on Tuesday, hours after a sheriff's report said he had pain pills in his pocket and showed signs of impairment at a crash last week.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="zxx" dir="ltr"><a href="https://t.co/T70784akfW">pic.twitter.com/T70784akfW</a></p>— Tiger Woods (@TigerWoods) <a href="https://twitter.com/TigerWoods/status/2039110644255891907?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 31, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The online court docket for Martin County, Florida, showed Woods entered a written plea of not guilty and planned to waive his appearance during an arraignment hearing next month.</p><p>Woods' eyes were bloodshot and glassy, his pupils dilated and he had opioid pills on him when interviewed at the scene of the crash, according to the arrest report released by the Martin County Sheriff's Office.</p><p>Woods' movements were slow and lethargic, he was sweating as he talked to deputies and told them he had taken prescription medication earlier in the morning, according to the report. Woods told deputies he had been looking at his phone and fiddling with the radio before he clipped a truck in front of him, the report said.</p><p>Deputies found two white pills, which were identified as the opioid hydrocodone used to treat pain, in his pocket, the report said.</p><p>When asked by a deputy if he took any prescription medications, Woods said, "I take a few."</p><p>Woods' defense attorney, Douglas Duncan, didn't respond to an email and phone call after the plea was entered on Tuesday.</p><p>Woods' agent at Excel Sports, Mark Steinberg, has not responded to multiple messages seeking comment.</p><p>The golfer was traveling at high speeds on a beachside, residential road on Jupiter Island when his Land Rover clipped the truck and rolled onto its side, according to the sheriff's office, which noted Woods showed signs of impairment.</p><p>The truck had $5,000 in damage, according to the report.</p><p>The truck driver and another person helped Woods out of his vehicle, with the golfer needing to climb out from the passenger side. Neither Woods nor the truck driver were injured.</p><p>During a field sobriety test, deputies noticed Woods limping and that he had a compression sock over his right knee. The golfer explained he had undergone seven back surgeries and over 20 leg operations and that his ankle seizes up while walking. Woods, who was hiccupping during the questioning, continuously moved his head during one of the sobriety tests and deputies had to instruct him several times to keep his head straight, the report said.</p><p>"Based on my observations of Woods, how he performed the exercises and based on my training, knowledge, and experience, I believed that Woods normal faculties were impaired, and he was unable to safely operate the motor vehicle," the deputy wrote after the tests.</p><p>Woods, 50, is the most influential figure in golf and has become as recognizable as any athlete in the world. The first person of Black heritage to win the Masters in 1997, he has captivated golf fans with records likely never to be broken.</p><p>But his injuries kept him from accomplishing more, including those suffered in a 2021 car crash that damaged his right leg so badly he said doctors considered amputation.</p><p>At this latest crash, Woods agreed to a Breathalyzer test that showed no signs of alcohol, but he refused a urine test, authorities said. He was arrested and released on bail eight hours later.</p><p>No one from Woods' camp or the PGA Tour — he is on the board and is chairman of the committee reshaping the competition model — have commented since his arrest.</p><p>Woods, who has been involved in many crashes over the years, is charged with driving under the influence with property damage and refusal to submit to a lawful test. He is scheduled for arraignment April 23.</p><p>Under a change to Florida law last year, refusing a law enforcement officer's request to take a breath, blood or urine test became a misdemeanor, even for a first offense.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Cubs right-hander Jameson Taillon delivers 'decent' start in Cubs' 2-0 loss to Angels
entries.summary: <p>Cubs right-hander Jameson Taillon’s first start of the season Tuesday wasn’t perfect, but it was a step in the right direction after a poor spring training.</p><p>In the Cubs’ 2-0 loss to the Angels, Taillon more resembled the pitcher he has been throughout his career. He allowed two hits, walked four and struck out three in 4⅔ scoreless innings.</p><p>In spring training, Taillon posted a 17.55 ERA with 12 strikeouts and nine walks in five starts. The results were startling enough that he had to reconsider what he was doing on the mound.</p><p>After the game against the Angels, Taillon said he just wanted to get out of Arizona and back to Wrigley Field.</p><p>‘‘It felt good to just get back out there and start the year on a decent note,’’ Taillon said.</p><p>Most veteran starters use spring training to work on their arsenals, sharpen a pitch or get stretched out. That Taillon felt the need to tinker with his delivery was a sound-the-alarm moment for him and the Cubs.</p><p>Aside from right-hander Edward Cabrera, who dazzled in his Cubs debut Monday, and right-hander Cade Horton, who allowed two runs and four hits in 6⅓ innings Saturday, the starting pitching has been a bit suspect early on. Opening Day starter Matthew Boyd had a shaky first outing, fellow left-hander Shota Imanaga allowed four runs Sunday and Taillon entered his first start with question marks after his subpar spring.</p><p>Taillon’s spring struggles raised questions about whether the Cubs have enough starting pitching to make a run deep into October. Their offseason need for starting pitching was so great that they traded prized outfield prospect Owen Caissie to the Marlins for Cabrera.</p><p>But even if Cabrera and Horton are on the right track, the Cubs will need Boyd, Imanaga and Taillon to give them quality outings, as well.</p><p>Taillon dealt with traffic on the bases early. With two outs and a runner on third in the first inning, Taillon struck out Jorge Soler on a 1-2 sweeper.</p><p>In the second, Taillon allowed a one-out single to Jo Adell and walked Josh Lowe on five pitches. But he maneuvered out of potential trouble by getting Logan O’Hoppe to pop out and Oswald Peraza to ground out.</p><p>Trouble found Taillon again in the fifth, when he allowed a one-out walk to O’Hoppe and a line-drive double to Peraza. He struck out Zach Neto after a seven pitch at-bat before intentionally walking Mike Trout. Left-hander Hoby Milner came on to face left-handed-hitting Nolan Schanuel and got him to ground out on an 0-2 sinker.</p><p>‘‘[Taillon] navigated [the traffic], and then there was very little hard contact,’’ manager Craig Counsell said.</p><p>Taillon’s biggest strengths are his dependability and consistency. He doesn’t have the velocity of Cabrera or Horton, but he wins with guile and incorporates many pitches to keep hitters off-balance. That’s why the number of hard-hit balls he allowed in spring training was alarming.</p><p>The average velocity on Taillon’s fastball against the Angels was down 1.5 mph from his average fastball last season, a continuation from spring training. He has been trying to recapture that velocity, which he said led to many of his spring struggles.</p><p>Instead, Taillon leaned on his cutter, which wasn’t successful for him last season.</p><p>‘‘Hopefully as the weather warms up and we keep working, [the velocity will] come,’’ Taillon said. ‘‘But I just needed to get back to executing and finding a way to get big-league hitters out.’’</p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Trump plans to attend Wednesday’s Supreme Court hearing on his bid to limit birthright citizenship
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump plans to sit in on Wednesday’s Supreme Court hearing on <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/supreme-court-birthright-citizenship-trump-immigration-83f337731f20247b7a300173da571c5f" target="_blank" >birthright citizenship</a>, making him the first sitting president to attend oral arguments at the nation’s highest court.</p><p>The Republican president’s official schedule, sent out by the White House, included a stop at the Supreme Court, where justices will hear Trump’s appeal of a lower court ruling that struck down his executive order limiting birthright citizenship.</p><p>The order, which Trump signed on the first day of his second term, declared that children born to parents who are in the United States illegally or temporarily are not American citizens. It’s an about-face from the long-standing view that the Constitution’s 14th Amendment and federal law since 1940 confer citizenship to everyone born on American soil, with narrow exceptions.</p><p>It’s not the first time Trump has considered showing up for a high court hearing. Last year, <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/trump-bessent-tariffs-trade-supreme-court-53952b451af79999c5f74d079b5c7652" target="_blank" >Trump said that he badly wanted</a> to attend a hearing on whether he overstepped federal law with his sweeping tariffs, but he decided against it, saying it would have been a distraction.</p><p>On Tuesday, however, Trump seemed more sure he’d be in court for Wednesday’s hearing while he spoke with reporters in the Oval Office.</p><p>“I’m going,” Trump said, when the upcoming arguments in the birthright citizenship case were mentioned. To a follow-up question clarifying that he planned to go in person, Trump said, “I think so, I do believe.”</p><p>Trump went to the Supreme Court in his first term for the <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/general-news-supreme-court-of-the-united-states-caa40e1d3878439cb2b0e492bf68fc33" target="_blank" >ceremonial swearing-in</a> of the first justice he appointed, Neil Gorsuch. Two other justices he appointed — Brett Kavanaugh and Amy Coney Barrett — also sit on the court.</p><p>Other presidents have dealt directly with the court, but don’t appear to have done so while in office. Richard Nixon argued a case between his time as vice president and president, and William Howard Taft served as chief justice after his presidency.</p><p>Trump, asked to whom he would be listening most closely, went on a lengthy detour Tuesday describing a court he viewed as mostly partisan, between justices appointed by Republican and Democratic presidents.</p><p>“I love a few of them,” he said. “I don’t like some others.”</p><p>The citizenship restrictions are a part of Trump’s broader immigration crackdown, but they have not yet taken effect anywhere in the country after being blocked by several courts.</p><p>A definitive ruling from the Supreme Court is expected by early summer.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Bad defense sinks Fedde, White Sox in 9-2 loss to Marlins
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> center fielder <b>Luisangel Acuna</b> is learning his position on the job, and the growing pains showed in an ugly defensive inning that cost his team mightily en route to a 9-2 loss to the Marlins.</p><p>The Sox peppered Miami starter <b>Janson Junk</b> for four singles in the third, with RBI from <b>Munetaka Murakami</b> — who has started his career on a five-game hitting streak — and <b>Andrew Benintendi</b>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Munetaka Murakami has recorded a hit in each of his first five career games! <a href="https://t.co/5aGmiunXlF">pic.twitter.com/5aGmiunXlF</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2039120568725774788?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 31, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p><b>Erick Fedde’s</b> return to the Sox’ rotation was near-flawless until the fourth inning, when Marlins second baseman <b>Xavier Edwards </b>singled and <b>Agustin Ramirez</b> sent a rocket to the right-field gap for a double that almost resulted in a full-speed collision between Acuna and Tristan Peters.</p><p>“I called for the ball, but he couldn’t hear me,” Acuna said.</p><p>A diving Murakami couldn’t stop a scorching <b>Liam Hicks</b> liner down the first-base line for a two-run double.</p><p>Then former Cub <b>Owen Caissie</b> knocked in another, advanced on a throwing error by Acuna and came home on a single from <b>Heriberto Hernandez</b>. Acuna, the Sox’ <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/01/20/white-sox-trade-luis-robert-jr-to-the-mets" target="_blank" ><u>main trade return</u></a> and replacement for <b>Luis Robert Jr.</b>, made another ill-advised throw on that play.</p><p>“I just tried to stay aggressive. My throw got away a little bit,” said Acuna, who was mostly an infielder with the Mets.</p><p>The Marlins tagged reliever <b>Bryan Hudson</b> for two more in the seventh, one on a fielding error charged to catcher <b>Edgar Quero</b> on a sacrifice bunt, and they piled the rest on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/26/jedixson-paez-white-sox-rookie-roster-rule-5" target="_blank" >Rule 5 rookie</a> <b>Jedixson Paez</b>.</p><p>In his first start with the Sox since <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/21/white-sox-starter-erick-feddes-stressful-offseason-led-him-back-to-where-he-needed-to-be" target="_blank" ><u>returning to the team</u></a> on a one-year deal, Fedde lasted five innings, giving up six hits and three earned runs with a walk and four strikeouts.</p><h3>Ben there</h3><p>Benintendi thought he’d be playing in a winning clubhouse a lot sooner when he signed the biggest contract in Sox history in 2023.</p><p>The Sox thought they’d be getting more from the former All-Star and Gold Glover, whose limited range after two years of Achilles issues was exposed during a tough opening series at Milwaukee.</p><p>Barring a trade — unlikely given the $30 million-plus remaining he’s still owed — Benintendi’s still got this season and next to wait for any potential good times to roll in.</p><p>“I think there are definitely pieces here for sure that will help the team in the future,” the 31-year-old said. “It’s not going to happen overnight.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7f0000" name="image-7f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Everson Pereira scores during the Sox’ first win of the season Monday in Miami.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Marta Lavandier/AP Photos</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Pereira rarin’ to go</h3><p>Right fielder <b>Everson Pereira</b> sat Tuesday, a night after taking a tumble while trying to track down a fly ball against the wall at loanDepot Park that went for a triple.</p><p>“He’s a little sore, and that’s probably exaggerating,” manager <b>Will Venable</b> said. “He fought me on it last night.”</p><p>Acquired in the offseason from Tampa Bay, Pereira has four hits in his first four games with the Sox.</p><p>“Just trying to slow the game down and breathe,” the 24-year-old said.</p><h3>Down on the farm</h3><p>After <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/28/white-sox-prospects-noah-schultz-sam-antonacci-triple-a-opening-day" target="_blank" ><u>impressive season debuts</u></a> from top pitching prospects <b>Hagen Smith</b> and <b>Noah Schultz</b> at Triple-A Charlotte, two more arms expected in Chicago sometime this year have turned in solid outings, too.</p><p>The 6-5 power righty <b>Tanner McDougal</b> struck out eight over four innings of work Saturday, giving up a run on two hits with four walks. And <b>Duncan Davitt</b>, acquired in last year’s <b>Adrian Houser</b> trade with the Rays, followed the next day with seven strikeouts and three walks over four innings, surrendering two runs.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Miami homecoming gives Miguel Vargas strong foundation to step up in White Sox clubhouse
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — The sheer volume of raucous cheers inside cavernous loanDepot Park this week has belied modest Miami crowd sizes and even the team receiving those ovations.</p><p>There were only 6,515 fans there to watch Miguel Vargas walk to the plate with the bases loaded in the fourth inning Monday. But from the roar that followed the ball he cracked into the Florida night, you might’ve thought Vargas had clubbed one 402 feet into the left-field bleachers at Rate Field — or to a home crowd in his native Havana.</p><p>Then again, they’re all home games down here for Vargas, the Cuban-born Miami resident who’s just as beloved by his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> teammates and a front office that’s looking for him to take a significant step in his development as a clubhouse leader and a key cog of their organizational rebuild.</p><p>“You’re not supposed to have favorites, but as a human, as a person, he is one of my favorites,” gushed manager Will Venable, who equally adores Vargas’ versatility in the field and in the batting order. “He’s just such a personable guy. He’s so caring.”</p><p>Arriving on the South Side in the Michael Kopech trade just in time for the worst days of the 2024 season’s record-breaking futility, Vargas’ numbers over his first full season last year were solid, if not necessarily screaming “franchise building block.” He hit .234/.316/.401 with 16 homers and 32 doubles in 138 games.</p><p>And the 26-year-old is off to a quality start at the plate in Year 2, if the grand slam <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/30/white-sox-vargas-grand-slam-first-win-season" target="_blank" ><u>didn’t give it away</u></a>. Through five games, Vargas is 5-for-17 with a double and two walks. He also has been steady on defense, entering the year as the closest thing to an every-day third baseman for perpetual lineup shuffler Venable.</p><p>Vargas got his first homer of the year while playing first base, and he has no small ambitions wherever he lines up. Just ask him about his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/22/white-sox-miguel-vargas-working-hard-to-become-complete-third-baseman" target="_blank" ><u>desire to win a Gold Glove</u></a>.</p><p>“Definitely trying to be the best I can,” said Vargas, who played as a teenager for the Industriales de La Habana in the Cuban National League.</p><div class="Enhancement" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Miguel Vargas (left) and Austin Hays celebrate after defeating the Miami Marlins Monday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Marta Lavandier/AP </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>His father, Lazaro Vargas, won two Olympic gold medals with Cuba in the 1990s, adding to the interest in Miguel’s career back home. English-speaking reporters are outnumbered in the clubhouse this week by Spanish-speaking media catching up with Vargas and other Sox with local ties, such as catcher Edgar Quero and rookie reliever Jedixson Paez, along with the sizable Japanese press corps following Vargas’ new friend and teammate Munetaka Murakami.</p><p>Vargas took his fellow first/third baseman under his wing in spring training and borrowed Murakami’s first-base mitt Monday.</p><p>“We’ve been doing a really good job, especially trusting the guy next to you, behind you, getting on base and doing the right things for winning baseball games,” Vargas said. “I think we’re in the right spot right now.”</p><p>After his six-RBI night — which put him in an early tie as the MLB leader entering play Tuesday — Vargas caught up with dozens of relatives and friends in the stands. “They’ve been supporting me my entire career, and I love playing in front of them,” he said.</p><p>This stage of his career finds Vargas evolving into a team leader, Venable said. “Once you establish yourself in this league, you get a little more time under your belt, you perform a little better, your vocal leadership has the ability to grow a little more.”</p><p>Another new teammate, Austin Hays, said he recognized that when he walked in the clubhouse.</p><p>“He’s one of those guys, he’s like a light anywhere he goes,” the veteran outfielder said of Vargas. “You’ve got to have guys like that in the clubhouse. He’s definitely one of those guys that is always going to keep everybody up and keep everybody going.”</p><p>But it’s not just the charisma.</p><p>“He’s got a lot of power to all sides of the field, he’s got pretty good plate discipline,” Hays said. “I think he can put up some really good numbers.”</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, count=9789, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, carouselAutoPlay=false, pullQuoteIcon=[{image=null, narrowImage=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=null}], promoTitleFont=font-2, ctaButtonText=[], primaryTextColor=#2a2a2a, buttonTextColor=#ffffff, linkTextColor=#1a1a1a, hideTitles=false, footerLinkColor=#ffffff, items=[{type=Image, _styledTemplate=null, publishDate=March 30, 2026 09:36 PM, imageAlignment=null, categoryUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox, isFeatured=false, authors=[{jobTitle=[{items=[Reporter/assistant editor]}], bio=[{items=[Mitchell Armentrout is a staff reporter covering government and politics from Chicago to Springfield, focusing on the expansion of gambling across Illinois as well as the Chicago Bears’ quest for a new stadium. entries.title: Horoscope for Wednesday, April 01, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The Full Moon in Libra peaks at 9:12 p.m.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today the full moon is opposite your sign, which could create concerns about long-term committed relationships. You might question how you function in them. You might question how you got involved. How would you finish the sentence "What people want out of me is —"?</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today you're concerned with your work and your health because of the energy of the full moon. It's important for you to feel useful and productive. Do you think your job is something where you should get by with as little as possible? Or do you think you're always working for yourself and your own self-respect?</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today's full moon might create problems dealing with your kids, a romantic relationship, or it might even make you doubt your creative talents. Why not pursue activities that give you joy? Life is full of a string of little pleasures. It doesn't have to be all or nothing.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You are a moon child, and today's full moon might make you feel caught between the demands of home and family versus those of your career or external world. You can't please both. For this particular full moon, you can't ignore your career or authority figures.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Pay attention to everything you say and do today because this is a mildly accident-prone day due to the full moon energy. Communication is important to you today. You might feel you have to say something or share an idea with someone. Or perhaps no one will listen? Go gently.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> The full moon today takes place in your money house, which might trigger disputes with others about money, earnings or something you own. You might even argue about your values, especially as they relate to shared property and shared responsibilities. Stay calm.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You like harmony in your surroundings. Although you’re an excellent debater, you don’t like confrontations. Today is the only full moon in your sign all year, which can create difficulty with partners and close friends. Be aware of this and tread carefully.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today's full moon energy might create disputes with co-workers, health care workers, or a pet. Basically, every full moon is in opposition to the sun, which means these two energies are "fighting." It lasts about 48 hours.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You might encounter some difficulty dealing with friends and groups today, especially if they are related to your kids, a vacation, a social situation or anything to do with sports and education. It's hard to reconcile opposing ideas. Relax. You don't have to be right; just be a caring person.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-290000" name="html-embed-module-290000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>so-so</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today, a public argument might take place in which you're involved. It could be noticeable, or it might be a whispered exchange in an elevator. Whatever the case, today's full moon might aggravate your dealings with bosses, parents and authority figures. Stay chill.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Steer clear of controversial subjects today because the full moon will only exacerbate things. People feel heated emotions today that override their logic. Therefore, your best bet is to walk on by. Don't make waves.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Disputes or differences of opinion about shared property, taxes, debt, insurance issues, as well as shared responsibilities, are likely today because of the full moon. Don't get caught up in this! Keep your head down and your powder dry.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Mackenzie Davis (1987) shares your birthday today. You are dignified, wise and quietly confident. You're also positive, organized and energetic. This year is about service, perhaps to family. Because you might be a support for those who turn to you for guidance, comfort and courage, it's important to take care of yourself. Time for a makeover?</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Blackhawks encouraged by third-period play in overtime loss to Jets
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> felt like they righted their ship at home Tuesday after capsizing on their road trip, but their overtime weakness prevented them from translating it into a win.</p><p>Jets star Kyle Connor's game-winning goal sent the Hawks to a 4-3 overtime loss — their fourth consecutive defeat — and dropped their record in overtime/shootout games this season to 7-14.</p><p>"We haven't been good enough three-on-three, so that's frustrating for all of us," coach Jeff Blashill said. "But the process we had through lots of that game was really good."</p><p>The Hawks are running low on time to get to 30 wins, a reasonable target for their step forward this year. They have 27 wins with seven games left. A few more overtime victories obviously would've made a difference.</p><p>The Hawks were solid in the first period and terrible in the second Tuesday, following a similar pattern as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/29/blackhawks-devils-spencer-knight-frank-nazar-defensive-issues" target="_blank" >in recent games</a>. They broke that pattern with one of their best third periods of the season, though, rallying back from a 3-1 deficit to at least earn a point.</p><p>The forward line of rookie Anton Frondell and veteran Tyler Bertuzzi (plus Ilya Mikheyev) scored all three goals, including the equalizer with 10:14 left. </p><p>Frondell earned his first goal in the NHL, and Bertuzzi now leads the team with a new career high of 31 goals. Nonetheless, Frondell also made defensive misreads on the Jets' first and last goals.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Tyler Bertuzzi with a highlight-reel goal for his 30th of the season, which ties a career-high. Wow, what an effort. <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/Blackhawks?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#Blackhawks</a> <a href="https://t.co/iTuYU8dzXq">pic.twitter.com/iTuYU8dzXq</a></p>— Charlie Roumeliotis (@CRoumeliotis) <a href="https://twitter.com/CRoumeliotis/status/2039161530160169307?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 1, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3><b>Injury updates</b></h3><p>Hawks defensemen <b>Artyom Levshunov </b>and <b>Matt Grzelcyk </b>are out for the season, Blashill said. They won’t return from their injuries before the season finale April 15.</p><p>For Grzelcyk (upper-body injury), this probably marks the end of his one-year tenure with the Hawks. He panned out well as a training-camp tryout, providing steady depth. He had 12 assists but didn’t score in 69 appearances.</p><p>For Levshunov (<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/29/blackhawks-artyom-levshunov-injury-hand-fracture-kevin-korchinski" target="_blank" >left hand</a>), his tumultuous rookie season — which somehow yielded many encouraging and discouraging indications about his future potential at the same time — is over.</p><p>Forward <b>Andrew Mangiapane</b>, who missed his seventh consecutive game, will likely return this season. He skated individually Monday.</p><p><b>Sacha Boisvert </b>also sat out as a healthy scratch after making his first three NHL appearances on the recent road trip. Blashill clarified Boisvert won’t ‘‘play every single game, but he’ll play a good number of them’’ down the stretch.</p><h3><b>Toews’ question</b></h3><p>Mediocre play from all the teams on the Western Conference playoff bubble has allowed the Jets to climb back into the race, which means former Hawks captain <b>Jonathan Toews</b>’ attention is squarely focused on that right now.</p><p>The big question for him is whether he will play again next season, but an answer on that front is unlikely to come until the Jets’ hopes are dashed.</p><p>There was less pomp and circumstance for Toews’ second game back in Chicago than there was for his first Jan. 19, but he still received a loud round of applause during "O Canada" and another during his first shift.</p><h3><b>New builder</b></h3><p>Beloved former Hawks player and broadcaster <b>Troy Murray</b>, who died March 7 of cancer, was added to the inaugural class of the Blackhawks Hall of Fame.</p><p>Murray will be inducted as a ‘‘builder,’’ a category also used by the Hockey Hall of Fame to recognize people who substantially contributed to the game off the ice.</p><p>Eleven other former players — the nine with retired jerseys, plus <b>Duncan Keith </b>and <b>Steve Larmer </b>— make up the rest of the inaugural class, which will be inducted April 10 and then honored April 11 against the Blues.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Dolores Huerta and farmworkers overshadowed by Cesar Chavez finally get their due
entries.summary: <p>As someone who was personally involved with the United Farm Workers and met with Cesar Chavez, the heartbreaking and disgusting <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/03/18/allegations-of-abuse-by-cesar-chavez-sadden-shock-chicagos-latino-community" >stories about the union’s co-founder </a>are revolting and deeply troubling. The abuse and rape allegations against the now departed Chavez deserve full treatment and exposure. </p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/18/us/cesar-chavez-sexual-abuse-allegations-ufw.html?unlocked_article_code=1.XFA.I-Ee.Roln972MB3QJ&smid=url-share" target="_blank" >New York Times</a> thoroughly reported the union leadership's chauvinism and the allegations that were ignored to protect the leader and the movement.</p><p>UFW co-founder Dolores Huerta said in a <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/18/us/dolores-huerta-statement-cesar-chavez.html?unlocked_article_code=1.XFA.jWNi.oNwC4Z-ZFCBH&smid=url-share" target="_blank" >statement</a> published by The New York Times: “I will continue my commitments to workers, as well as my commitment to women’s rights, to make sure we have a voice and that our communities are treated with dignity and given the equity that they have so long been denied. I have kept this secret long enough. My silence ends here.”</p><p>Huerta is correct. The emphasis should be on the workers, especially the women, who deserve a full voice and deep respect. Every movement, no matter how valiant or aspirational, should reflect its values both internally and externally. Chavez and the union’s leadership team failed, and there is no excuse.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-220000" name="module-220000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>The farmworker movement brought national attention to the too often forgotten farmworkers, the people whose hard labor fills our tables. Huerta is a critical voice, and it is painful to learn about the secrets that troubled her soul as she rallied the nation for human rights. The farmworkers' struggle for human decency inspired and shaped my young adult years.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f50000" name="image-f50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3cefc45/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1090x612+0+103/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F8b%2F2972443c47a38ef860a19fa28aaa%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-30-at-12-48-43-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3787ccb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1090x612+0+103/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F8b%2F2972443c47a38ef860a19fa28aaa%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-30-at-12-48-43-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a44804f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1090x612+0+103/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F8b%2F2972443c47a38ef860a19fa28aaa%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-30-at-12-48-43-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Three people, a man and two women, pose for a photo. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a44804f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1090x612+0+103/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F8b%2F2972443c47a38ef860a19fa28aaa%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-30-at-12-48-43-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2a9b50d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1090x612+0+103/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F8b%2F2972443c47a38ef860a19fa28aaa%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-30-at-12-48-43-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a44804f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1090x612+0+103/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F8b%2F2972443c47a38ef860a19fa28aaa%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-30-at-12-48-43-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>An undated photo of Mike Matejka and his wife, Kari Sandhaas, with United Farm Workers co-founder Dolores Huerta (center). </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In September 1973, I was a student at Illinois State University. As a good Catholic, I attended the Newman Center for Sunday Mass, where we were encouraged to support the UFW and the grape, lettuce and Gallo wine boycotts. The UFW had endured a bloody summer, with multiple beatings, arrests and deaths of farmworker activists in California. Workers left the Golden State to share their stories and build the boycott.</p><p>That month, we held a rally for the UFW on the ISU Quad. UFW organizer Gustavo Gutierrez and two women shared their stories. </p><p>I was deeply touched by their stories about working in the fields and their hopes for a better life through unionization. I became a UFW activist and helped organize pickets, marches and even the Newman Center choir as rally singers in Chicago. </p><p>Cesar Chavez was the public figure, but it was the basic dignity these workers displayed that inspired me. They reminded me of my own working-class family and our immigrant roots, striving for a better life and respect.</p><h3>Women rallied for change downstate</h3><h3></h3><p>In 1981, nursery workers in Onarga, a village 90 miles south of Chicago, wanted to organize. It was the farmworker women who led this effort. Maria Elena, Gloria Chiquita (“Little Gloria”) and Gloria Grande (“Tall Gloria”) all became fast friends. We tried to find medical care for Don Lupito, a slight, skinny man, with persistent ulcerous wounds on his legs, which he believed were from pesticide exposure. We gathered donations for the Illinois Farm Worker Service Center in Onarga. Chavez came to dedicate the center.</p><p>As the Reagan era dawned, the UFW was barely surviving in California. Yet the service center persisted because of those women. When the Illinois AFL-CIO asked what labor could do, they requested clean drinking water and portable toilets in the fields. The <a class="Link" href="https://www.illinoislaborhistory.org/hall-of-honor-articles/2015?utm_source=chatgpt.com" target="_blank" >Illinois Field Sanitation Act</a> resulted.</p><p>What Chavez did was shameful. Historians and advocates will rightfully probe the criminal allegations against him and the union executive board’s misogynistic failure to challenge his leadership. </p><p>Yet this is a moment to ask ourselves who still picks the lettuce and grapes, thins the broccoli and brings that bounty to our tables? Many are undocumented and live in fear of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. They are the neglected workers in our vast food system who are exposed to pesticides and do backbreaking, vital labor, and are proud of their contributions to our lives.</p><p>When it's peach harvest time in Anna, Illinois, or melon season in the sandy soil outside Havana, Illinois, you’ll find farmworkers laboring. Metro Chicago has more agricultural workers than other parts of Illinois — they tend nurseries and landscape or mow lawns. When we consider Chavez, let’s not forget the workers, especially the women, still ignored and forgotten. What Chavez did was shameful and criminal; let’s focus on the workers who deserve dignity and decency.</p><p><i>Mike Matejka, of Normal, is a retired member of the Laborers International Union of North America Local 362 in Bloomington. For 40 years, he edited the Grand Prairie Union News for the Bloomington & Normal Trades & Labor Assembly. He was the governmental affairs director for the Great Plains Laborers District Council for 22 years.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b00000" name="module-b00000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Mike Matejka

entries.title: Chicago visitor moved by 'No Kings' rally: 'Always try to stand on the right side of history'
entries.summary: <p>Sometimes history begins to write itself in the street and in the people who dare to step out holding signs that read, "Stop war on Iran!" or "No war" or "This is not OK."</p><p>On that same street, beneath a green traffic light, another sign suddenly takes on a different meaning: "Begin one way." I look at it and feel it says much more than signaling the beginning of a unidirectional street. </p><p>One of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/28/no-kings-rallies-kick-off-in-evanston-cicero-and-lincoln-square-as-hundreds-expected-to-protest-in-chicago" >“No Kings”</a> rally signs I saw this weekend in Chicago read, "The power of the people is greater than the people in power." Another sign, a warning that echoes strongly, said,"Don’t make me repeat myself. Signed: History."</p><p>I am just a visitor. It is not my country. It is not my war. Yet I am left with a heavy heart, because there is something that belongs to all of us: the ability to recognize injustice.</p><p>History does not always repeat itself, but we glance at it sideways, aware that too often we end up doing what we already know is wrong.</p><p>Today, as so many times before, some raise their voices. Others of us watch, moved, aware that this is not a distant conflict.</p><p>Perhaps the path forward is not only for those holding the signs, but also for those who stop being spectators — like the young woman to my left who suddenly began to cry; or the older woman who, after talking about blues music, felt the need to tell me that this is not good for anyone.</p><p>Stop war. Begin one way.</p><p>May we always try to stand on the right side of history.</p><p><i>Lidia López García,</i> <i>Madrid, Spain</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-210000" name="module-210000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Cut delivery robots some slack</b></h3><p>Dear Chicago,</p><p>You can't make an omelet without breaking some eggs, and you certainly can't deliver an omelet without <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/25/delivery-robot-smashes-into-old-town-bus-shelter-in-second-crash-of-the-week" target="_blank" >destroying public property</a>. Fortunately, we at Coco and Serve robotics have committed ourselves to safety and our patented design keeps your meal safe from all debris.</p><p>Please keep in mind that while our recent shard-ridden peccadilloes with assorted bus shelters are indeed unfortunate, we are technology pioneers bravely trailblazing an incredible future where you can have food from a nearby restaurant brought to your doorstep (well, near your doorstep. In fact, actual doorsteps-or steps of any kind, really-present a bit of a problem, but with another funding round I'm sure we can solve it). The point is this is a new paradigm, and you, Chicago, should be grateful to have a front row seat to our collective ascendance! This is disruption. It’s uncomfortable, but like pretty much all other technology it’s gonna be great and awesome and definitely work exactly the way it’s supposed to with no unforeseen consequences.</p><p>Frankly, I don’t understand the ire we delivery robots seem to receive. We are out here every day doing something that no one has ever done before, something no one else can do. If we aren't delivering stuff, then who? So we broke a bus shelter or two. I honestly don’t understand why that's a big deal. If anything, it just shows the tireless dedication we have getting burritos to your home or office (or, again, as near as we can get to it, you’ll have to come outside if there are steps), and I can’t stress enough how little glass was in the food!</p><p>Here in Silicon Valley we move fast and break things, and I don’t even move that fast, so I’m not even doing the whole thing, and I wish you all would just relax because your food is here! Can you come down and get it? There are steps.</p><p><i>Eddie Lehwald, Logan Square</i></p><h3>Getting the show on the road isn’t easy</h3><p>I enjoyed <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elly-fishman-for-wbez" target="_blank" >Elly Fishman</a>'s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/classical-music/2026/03/24/chicago-symphony-orchestra-tour-diary-flutist-emma-gerstein-motherhood-music" >piece</a> that ran in the Sun-Times about Chicago Symphony Orchestra flutist Emma Gerstein taking her baby on tour and writing about it in her diary. </p><p>I often comment on how the general public has no idea what musicians go through to perform their art. I've been playing music professionally since my days in the Lane Tech High School orchestra and band 62 years ago. I remember thinking during a study hall class at Lane Tech that "going on the road with a band or orchestra has to be the most fun thing in the world." </p><p>A few years later, I got to do short road trips playing with famous bandleaders and a few celebrity singers. When onstage and performing, it <i>was</i> the most fun thing. But the driving, rehearsals, etc., were hard work. And the CSO musicians have to always be in top musical form. </p><p>The Sun-Times article on Gerstein's experience during the seven-day tour was so well written that the readers almost felt like they were part of the tour. Thanks again for having an article about great musicians and a great orchestra.</p><p><i>Steven Cooper, La Grange Park</i></p><h3>Thanks for the voter support </h3><p>Since Sun-Times reader Larry Craig is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/03/30/landlords-rental-inspections-trump-tsa-dhs-immigration-loyola-capitol-riots-mail-in-ballots" >against mail-in ballots </a>and adamantly supports only in-person voting, I would like to ask Larry to take my 86-year-old sister — she'll turn 87 on Friday — to her polling place. But please be careful taking Connie down her front steps, and please do not take her in an SUV, as she isn't able to get up into the front seat. Hopefully, there isn't snow on the ground, as her wheelchair isn't equipped with snow tires.</p><p>My sister has been an educator all her working life and prides herself in carefully evaluating the candidates running for office who will uphold the Constitution of this great nation.</p><p>Larry, let me know your availability this November.</p><p><i>Tom Metz, Oriole Park</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: Daughter wrongly blames mom for calling the cops
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have a grown daughter who is most likely an alcoholic. I am not a doctor, so I really don't know. We live in different cities. A few years ago, she was sitting on the sidewalk outside her apartment dead drunk and holding a lit cigarette. Someone saw her passed out and called the police, who took her to jail. To this day, she accuses me of having called the police. It wasn't me. I didn't know what was happening. This makes me furious. I have no reason to lie to her.</p><p>The next morning, her boss called me looking for her. We found out later she had been arrested. After talking with her boss, I did phone the police to ask if maybe she was at the station. I live 30 miles away. I'm angry because I'm being falsely accused. I know I need to let this go, but she triggers me every time she brings it up, and it's been five years! Please help me. — <i>DRAMA MAMA IN TEXAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR DRAMA MAMA:</b> Whoever called the police to report that there was a woman passed out on the curb did your daughter a favor. She was vulnerable and was taken to safety. Whatever your daughter's problem is, whether it's alcohol, some other substance or mental health issues, she is clearly a troubled individual. When she accuses you, don't bother getting into an argument with her. Reiterate that whoever it was did her a favor and then change the subject.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I am a 35-year-old woman who's never been in any sort of relationship with a man and, yes, I'm a virgin. For some reason, I am surrounded by women at school and at work. The one time I tried dating a colleague, it turned into the office romance from hell. I was publicly rejected, and he ended up with another female colleague. Since then, there has been — nothing.</p><p>I'm resigned to not being in a relationship, but at this point, I want to stop being a virgin. I'm considering hiring an escort to help me with my issue in a last-ditch attempt to experience what it's like. I'm tired of waiting for that one man who will probably never show. Should I go ahead with my escort plan or keep waiting aimlessly like an idiot? — <i>ALONE AND CONFUSED IN NEW YORK</i></p><p><b>DEAR ALONE:</b> I recommend against it. For a man, this might be a solution. However, for women, it helps to have some feeling for one's partner for the experience to be satisfactory, and that element would be missing with an escort. Consider discussing this with a licensed therapist to understand why your romantic relationships may not be happening, because there is definitely a reason.</p><p><b>DEAR READERS:</b> At sundown, the first night of Passover begins. This major Jewish holiday celebrates the most momentous event in Jewish history — the liberation of the Jewish people from slavery in Egypt. Happy Passover to my readers who observe this important holiday. — <i>LOVE, ABBY</i></p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For everything you need to know about wedding planning, order "How to Have a Lovely Wedding." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Wedding Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: ‘The Burger Bible’ delves into the contentious, yet delicious history of America’s favorite handheld
entries.summary: <p><i>The following excerpt from “The Burger Bible: The Complete Lowdown on the Humble Hamburger (Welbeck)," by Maggie Hennessy, explores how the handheld marvel was actually a millennia in the making, despite more contemporary claims. The book publishes April 2 and includes profiles of 80 iconic burgers from around the globe. This excerpt is provided with permission from the author and publisher. </i></p><hr><p>Though the humble hamburger has been widely popular in the United States for only the last century and a quarter, one could argue — without the tracest hint of theatrics — that it represents the delicious culmination of millennia of culinary history, amassed in fits and starts from across the globe.</p><p>The burger’s origins stretch back as far as the earliest days of cattle domestication in Stone Age Mesopotamia and the ancient Chinese creation of jiaozi, or dumplings made with minced (ground) meat. By the 13th century, minced meat more overtly entered the gastronomic canon when the Mongols and Tatars were fighting in Mongolia.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a39ba4a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+1127/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff8%2Fbb%2Fa02273d14fd0894d2f4e5d25ac8f%2Fmaggie-summer2023-sandynoto-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0a559da/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+1127/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff8%2Fbb%2Fa02273d14fd0894d2f4e5d25ac8f%2Fmaggie-summer2023-sandynoto-01.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a39ba4a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+1127/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff8%2Fbb%2Fa02273d14fd0894d2f4e5d25ac8f%2Fmaggie-summer2023-sandynoto-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Burger Bible” author Maggie Hennessy</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Sandy Noto</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>According to George Motz, American burger historian and owner of the New York restaurant Hamburger America, the Tatars were particularly fond of raw mutton, and used to ride with it beneath their saddles all day. After they set up camp, they chopped up the raw, warm meat, added a handful of spices, and ate it just like that.</p><p>Minced mutton eventually made its way to ship workers and ports lining the Baltic Sea, which cleared a pathway for its westward push to parts of Europe, including Scandinavia. Eventually, it made its way to Germany and the port of Hamburg, when the dish also shifted from raw mutton to chopped cooked beef, known as frikadellen. It made for a cheap, tasty meal for German migrants awaiting their ships, and when they left for the United States in the mid-19th century, they brought that knowledge with them. Of course, frikadellen meant nothing to non-Germans living in the US at the time, supposedly prompting the name to be changed to “steak in the style of Hamburg” or, more plainly, “Hamburg steak.”</p><p>As German migrants moved westward to farm, state fairs also started popping up to educate folks about agriculture. German farmers set up stands peddling their Hamburg steak, which was considered quite exotic at the time. And it wouldn’t be long before someone got the bright idea to synthesize charred minced (ground) beef and bread. But who?</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-170000" name="image-170000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a96f4dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3240x1818+0+228/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F59%2Fee16e8514d5f90a31be9df46a9c8%2Fap16259041923077.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2f9b15d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3240x1818+0+228/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F59%2Fee16e8514d5f90a31be9df46a9c8%2Fap16259041923077.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c5f9318/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3240x1818+0+228/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F59%2Fee16e8514d5f90a31be9df46a9c8%2Fap16259041923077.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Travel-Trip-New Haven" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c5f9318/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3240x1818+0+228/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F59%2Fee16e8514d5f90a31be9df46a9c8%2Fap16259041923077.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9f5f355/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3240x1818+0+228/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F59%2Fee16e8514d5f90a31be9df46a9c8%2Fap16259041923077.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c5f9318/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3240x1818+0+228/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F59%2Fee16e8514d5f90a31be9df46a9c8%2Fap16259041923077.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The longtime Connecticut eatery Louis’ Lunch was recognized by the Library of Congress as the U.S. birthplace of the hamburger. Burgers are made on a small gas grill, and the restaurant does not serve ketchup. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Beth J. Harpaz/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>WHO INVENTED THE HAMBURGER SANDWICH?</b></h3><p>Like all good (and tall) tales, the hamburger sandwich has numerous origin stories and just as many folks who’d confidently poke holes in each of those claims. Even the 2016 recognition of Louis’ Lunch in New Haven, Connecticut, as the official “Birthplace of the Hamburger Sandwich” by the Library of Congress hasn’t quieted this long-raging debate despite proffering more written evidence than many claims based in oral history. All we know for sure is that the beefy handheld we know and love was born somewhere in America during the late 19th century.</p><p>The Connecticut-born story posits that a harried customer came into Louis’ Lunch one day, explaining that he was in a rush and asking for a meal to eat on the run. Owner Louis Lassen made a patty out of minced (ground) steak trimmings, grilled (broiled) it and then sandwiched it between two slices of white toast. If you need more proof, this fourth-generation, family-owned restaurant is not only still in operation, but still churning out its classic burgers on toast, which are cooked vertically in its three upright cast-iron grill (broiler) stoves dating back to 1898.</p><p>Of course, the state of Wisconsin begs to differ, claiming that back in 1885, Charlie Nagreen, who earned the moniker “Hamburger Charlie,” got the bright idea to squash the butter-sizzled meatballs he was selling at the Seymour Fair between two bread slices, for more portable snacking.</p><p>Meanwhile, in Athens, Texas, “Fletcher Davis,” known as Uncle Dave, supposedly invented the hamburger in the late 1880s, when he started peddling minced beef patties at his small café on the Henderson County courthouse square. Legend further has it that Uncle Dave took his sandwich to the 1904 World’s Fair, in St. Louis, where it was derisively nicknamed the “hamburger” by St. Louis citizens who viewed the practice of devouring (often raw) minced beef as barbaric — thus poking a hole in the aforementioned story of how the name “hamburger” came to be.</p><p>Texas is so sure of this version, by the way, that in 2007 the state legislature adopted a resolution naming Davis the hamburger sandwich’s inventor.</p><p>Wait a minute, though. Other reports tell us that around 1891 in Bowden, Oklahoma, Oscar Weber Bilby began grilling minced Angus meat on yeast buns — a claim that earned the Oklahoma governor’s support in 1995. Indiana has a pair of candidates, too, in Frank and Charles Menches, who replaced minced pork with minced beef patties in the sandwiches they were selling at the 1885 Erie County Fair.</p><p>Are you starting to see the problem?</p><p>Indeed, there’s no definitive way to prove who actually invented the burger as we now know it, as sure as its many claimants may seem. Luckily for us, threads still connect us to the burger’s rich American heritage in the form of restaurants that have stood the test of time, including Dyer’s Burgers in Memphis, Tennessee, which opened in 1912; Cozy Inn in Salina, Kansas, which debuted in 1922; Taylor’s Maid-Rite in Marshalltown, Iowa, which arrived in 1926; Miner-Dunn in Highland, Indiana, which opened in 1932; and White Manna, born in Hackensack, New Jersey.</p><p>Most of these timeworn storefronts are best reached by car, meaning you can experience their delicious, historical contributions the way generations before you have: by embarking on the Great American Road Trip, which itself came of age alongside the evolution of America’s favorite fast food meal. Plus, you should never get into a lively argument about burger origins on an empty stomach.</p><p><i>Excerpted with permission from "The Burger Bible: The Complete Lowdown on the Humble Hamburger (Welbeck)," by Maggie Hennessy.</i> </p>
entries.author: Maggie Hennessy | WBEZ

entries.title: ‘Burger Bible’ author picks her top 5 Chicago burgers
entries.summary: <p>For a city and country with a profound love of beef and melted cheese on a bun, the burger represents far more than salty sustenance. It is a beautiful, indulgent, messy, no doubt nostalgic moment in time.</p><p>I spent a chunk of 2025 writing a <a class="Link" href="https://www.barnesandnoble.com/w/the-burger-bible-maggie-hennessy/1147259203" target="_blank" ><u>book</u></a> on this ubiquitous, beloved handheld. Out April 2, it’s called “The Burger Bible,” and it traces the origins, lore and most beloved styles of this friendly handheld, culminating in 80-plus profiles of iconic, mouthwatering, viral and unique burgers from around the U.S. and beyond.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div id="Form-newsletter-wrapper" class="Form-newsletter-wrapper" data-module data-has-background ><a class="AnchorLink" id="custom-form-c10000" name="custom-form-c10000"></a><bsp-form class="Form-newsletter" data-form-container data-has-background target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>Thus, I should have anticipated my editor at WBEZ/Chicago Sun-Times asking me to whittle down my favorite Chicago burgers — a thorny subject for even the most hardened expert.</p><p>As one person doing her best to document our great, beefy metropolis, my contribution is far from the final word. There’s the matter of personal preferences. I’d rather eat a meatier patty than one smashed to the brink of becoming seared meat leather. I like balanced ratios and simple, piquant toppings — a good pickle, a shot of mustard, a sticky, plebian cheese.</p><p>There’s also the bread, an important aspect, if not more so than the rest. I profess an inordinate love of raw and griddled onions, the latter of which factor heavily in flavoring the likes of, say, the excellent Big Baby burger at <a class="Link" href="https://nickysalsip.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Nicky’s The Real McCoy</u></a> in Alsip, though nowhere more exemplary than Ragadan in Uptown.</p><p>Indeed, my all-time favorite Chicago burger remains the brawny Slagel Farms burger heaped with caramelized onions on potato bap at the bygone gastropub, Owen & Engine. (This beefy specter still teases us from time to time, when owner Bo Fowler pops up with an O&E menu at her modern brewpub <a class="Link" href="https://www.bixi.beer/" target="_blank" ><u>BiXi Beer</u></a>.)</p><p>Does all of this make me biased? Wildly subjective? Untrustworthy even? Absolutely. But isn't this the great joy of our collective burger obsession? Behold, here are the five Chicago burgers I love eating most. Want to share yours? Tell us about it at <a class="Link" href="mailto:arts@wbez.org" target="_blank" ><u>arts@wbez.org</u></a>, and we may add yours to a reader favorites list.</p><hr><p></p><div class="Enhancement" class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Charly’s Burger at </b><a class="Link" href="https://www.charlysburgers.com/" target="_blank" ><b><u>Charly’s Burgers</u></b></a></h3><p><i>Belmont-Cragin, 2320 N. Cicero Ave.</i></p><p>Entering this brightly lit storefront at the harried intersection of Cicero and Fullerton, your eye trains on a wild-eyed, grinning burger mural. Then, boom! It hits you: the gently smoky, beefy-scented sizzle of maybe 100 burgers cooking at once.</p><p>Charly’s positively cranks out patties from open to close, mostly for carryout since it contains just three coveted stools. The namesake double cheeseburger ($12 or $15 with fries and a drink) lives in the towering, sloppy-good burger realm, packing two six-ounce house-ground beef and bacon patties with a satisfying char, melted American cheese, a pile of caramelized onions and shaved pickles, and a generous smear of mayo-based secret sauce. Have I mentioned that the fries — lacy-crisp outside, fluffy within — count among my favorite anywhere in the city? You’ll want a side of cheese sauce for dipping.</p><hr><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink"
