class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El Centro Cultural Puertorriqueño históricamente ha realizado actividades de divulgación en clubes nocturnos y bares para encontrar a personas en riesgo, ofrecer pruebas de VIH y darles información sobre medicamentos y otros recursos. Pero sin financiamiento, eso tendrá que detenerse.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El Departamento de Salud de Chicago, que dice que solo ha recibido financiamiento federal parcial, confirmó que negó tres solicitudes del centro, pero proporcionará algunos fondos para programas de salud mental y vivienda de emergencia.</p><p>Ige, del departamento de salud de la ciudad, dijo que está buscando socios que coincidan con las áreas comunitarias que vieron aumentar los casos de VIH el año pasado y que también sean culturalmente competentes.</p><p>Un cambio a largo plazo para abordar cómo los latinos ven el VIH también deberá incluir servicios impulsados por la comunidad, como grupos de apoyo, dijo Alfredo Flores, director asociado de operaciones en CALOR, un grupo con sede en Chicago que proporciona servicios de VIH en español.</p><p>Él dice que la prevención se ha centrado principalmente en la atención del VIH y la PrEP, “y realmente no se ha enfocado en construir sistemas sostenibles para fomentar la educación, fomentar la construcción de comunidad y fomentar la educación comunitaria”.</p><p>Para Esmeralda Lozano, encargada de pruebas y consejera para el Centro Cultural Puertorriqueño, la incertidumbre sobre financiamiento es aterradora incluso para ella, ya que se preocupa por cómo continuará accediendo a la PrEP y a la atención médica que necesita como mujer trans. Pero eso no le impedirá intentar transmitir un mensaje positivo con quienes interactúa, destacando que las personas son dignas de atención médica.</p><p>“Estamos aquí para empoderarnos y simplemente aceptarnos”, destacó Lozano.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: MLB revives 'This Week in Baseball'
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — Major League Baseball is bringing back "This Week in Baseball" by giving it a digital flair.</p><p>The iconic series will be revived on Major League Baseball's X account, as well as @XOriginals, every Friday at 11 a.m. Chicago time and will run through the postseason.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/giAsugzxZIM?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="This Week in Baseball Closing Credits (1988)"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>"This Week in Baseball" debuted in 1977 and was a weekly 30-minute program produced by MLB that highlighted the major events of the past week. The show was hosted by the late Hall of Fame announcer Mel Allen for many years. It ran until 1998 and saw a second stint from 2000-11.</p><p>The new-look "TWIB" will have episodes ranging from 5 to 10 minutes. It will be produced by MLB studios and include many segments from its predecessor, including highlights, bloopers, player profiles and "TWIB Notes." Kait Maniscalco will serve as the host.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Bears increasingly unlikely to play internationally this season as NFL has yet to reach out about it
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — It’s unlikely <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >the Bears</a> will be selected to play in an international game this season, because while the NFL hasn’t announced all the matchups, it hasn’t approached them about it.</p><p>"Typically the league will feel you out on a particular destination [by now], and we haven't heard anything,” owner George McCaskey said Wednesday at the end of the NFL annual meeting.</p><p>The Bears already are assured of playing all their home games at Soldier Field, but could have been selected as the road team against the Lions in Munich or the Falcons in Madrid. The league typically avoids playing divisional games overseas, although it is doing so with a Rams home game against the 49ers in Melbourne this season, and some teams request their home game against the Bears to be excluded because the fan base travels so heavily, which is good for the local economy.</p><p>The Bears last played <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2024/10/13/bears-qb-caleb-williams-gets-a-bit-pissed-off-then-lights-up-jaguars-35-16-with-4-td-passes-in-london" >a home game in London against the Jaguars in 2024</a> and went there as the road team against the Raiders in 2019.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Death of 25-year-old Avondale man ruled homicide from assault
entries.summary: <p>The weekend death of man who was assaulted in Avondale in March has been ruled a homicide on Wednesday, officials said. </p><p>An autopsy determined Alexander C. Kavanowski, 25, died of blunt head trauma from an assault on March 24 at the intersection of Belmont and Harding avenues, according to the Cook County medical examiner's office. </p><p>Kavanowski, of the 4900 block of West Nelson Avenue in Avondale, was pronounced dead Saturday at 3:31 p.m. and his death was ruled a homicide, the medical examiner's office said.</p><p>Police had no information about the attack. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Breaking down Blackhawks rookie Anton Frondell's best NHL performance so far
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> coach Jeff Blashill has compared Anton Frondell to Jonathan Toews twice in two days.</p><p>That's a premature comparison, probably influenced by Toews visiting Tuesday. But it's high praise nonetheless. Blashill clearly isn't worried about keeping expectations low around his Swedish rookie.</p><p>Frondell's NHL introduction has indeed been impressive. He's the first Hawks rookie to tally five points in his first five games since Artemi Panarin in 2015 and only the third rookie league-wide to do that since 2022.</p><p>And his performance <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/31/blackhawks-jets-third-period-tyler-bertuzzi-jonathan-toews" target="_blank" >Tuesday</a> was probably his best yet.</p><p>While playing center for just the second time, his line with Tyler Bertuzzi and Ilya Mikheyev was primarily deployed against the Jets' top line of Mark Scheifele, Kyle Connor and Alex Iafallo. The Hawks dominated that matchup, outshooting the Jets 8-3 and outscoring them 3-1.</p><p>The shift that led up to Frondell's first-period goal demonstrated particularly well all the things he can do offensively.</p><p>He initially exited the defensive zone, entered the offensive zone and made a smooth move toward the net all by himself (going through three Jets), although he wasn't able to get a shot off.</p><p>Seconds later, he found a loose puck behind the net and passed to Mikheyev for a shot on goal. Then he retrieved the puck again near the half-wall and passed low-to-high to Louis Crevier for another shot attempt.</p><p>Then he curled back toward the slot, kept his head up, buried a centering pass from Mikheyev and celebrated with arms raised as a crowd of 18,726 erupted.</p><p>"Back on the road trip, when we played in Madison Square Garden and different arenas, I thought, 'Oh [wow], this is pretty good,'" Frondell said. "But they said, 'Wait til you get back at home.' I know what they mean by that now. It's a pretty good crowd here."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">ANTON FRONDELL'S FIRST NHL GOAL!!! 🚨 <a href="https://t.co/SkkwPyluAR">pic.twitter.com/SkkwPyluAR</a></p>— NHL (@NHL) <a href="https://twitter.com/NHL/status/2039143694230495446?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 1, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The offensive possession that led to Bertuzzi's third-period equalizer also began with Frondell winning a battle down low — something he has already done so many times in five games.</p><p>Jets defenseman Neal Pionk got between Frondell and the puck, but Frondell craftily whacked it between Pionk's legs and then spun off him to gain uncontested control.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Crafty play by Anton Frondell to win this puck battle before Bertuzzi’s third-period goal: <a href="https://t.co/gSkDGmyCIr">pic.twitter.com/gSkDGmyCIr</a></p>— Ben Pope’s Video Clips (@BenPopeCSTclips) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCSTclips/status/2039435073347272760?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 1, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Frondell has, however, also committed a fair number of defensive mistakes. Blashill has praised his defensive attentiveness, but it takes time to grasp a new system and new position.</p><p>When Jets defenseman Josh Morrissey cut toward the net early in the second period, Frondell peeled off, expecting either Crevier or Alex Vlasic to be covering the near post. They weren't, and Morrissey scored. Crevier had made a mistake, but Frondell should've noticed and adjusted accordingly.</p><p>"He came back to the bench and said, 'Should I have stayed with [Morrissey]?'" Blashill said. "I said, 'Yes.' So that’s a good thing that he understands those situations and will learn from them. To me, that’s the No. 1 thing."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Josh Morrissey - Winnipeg Jets (13) <a href="https://t.co/c1Hxl5pwXy">pic.twitter.com/c1Hxl5pwXy</a></p>— NHL Goal Videos (@NHLGoalVideos) <a href="https://twitter.com/NHLGoalVideos/status/2039203500769284156?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 1, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Kyle Connor's overtime winner largely fell on Frondell, too, for not monitoring and sticking with the Jets winger closely enough in three-on-three man coverage.</p><p>Still, Blashill seems altogether thrilled with the early results from his Frondell-at-center experiment. Frondell sticking there long-term would give the Hawks a fantastic forward backbone with Connor Bedard, Frank Nazar and him as a "one-two-three" punch of centers.</p><p>There are pros and cons to any arrangement. That one would put more pressure on prospects Nick Lardis and Roman Kantserov to pan out as top-six wingers and on general manager Kyle Davidson to acquire a couple more wingers via draft or trade.</p><p>Frondell isn't thinking about any of that, though. He's simply trying to adjust to the NHL, which he called "a whole new level" compared to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/21/blackhawks-anton-frondell-nhl-debut-connor-bedard-linemate-rebuild-success" target="_blank" >Swedish league</a>.</p><p>"Everything goes so fast," he said. "Everyone is a lot better. I still have a lot to learn. [I'll] just keep going and get better."</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" entries.title: The Munetaka Murakami Show heads to Chicago after brutal road trip to start White Sox’ season
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — There haven’t been many bright spots in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>’ first six games, but it’s usually been Munetaka Murakami putting on the show.</p><p>Now, the 26-year-old first baseman, who has made a powerful impression in his first week in the big leagues, will bring The Murakami Show to Rate Field Friday for the Sox’ home opener, which was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/01/white-sox-home-opener-postponed-rain" target="_blank" ><u>pushed back a day due to Thursday’s rainy forecast</u></a>.</p><p>Murakami has gotten an early taste of the misery all too familiar to Sox fans as his new squad closed out their 1-5 season-opening road trip with a 10-0 blowout loss to the Marlins, mustering just three hits during Sandy Alcantara's masterful complete-game shutout at loanDepot Park.</p><p>Sox starter Shane Smith threw one into center-field while trying to turn a double play in the first, and the situation devolved with four straight hits leading to a quick 4-0 Miami lead. The Marlins put the game out of reach by the end of the third inning, tagging Smith for eight hits and seven earned runs.</p><p>Murakami didn’t fare any better against Alcantara than most of his teammates, going 0-for-3 with two strikeouts to end a five-game on-base streak to start his career. But he’s got five hits including three homers plus four walks with one MLB week <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/27/munetaka-murakami-home-run-white-sox?555" target="_blank" ><u>under his belt</u></a>.</p><p>“It's been really impressive, just the quality of at-bats,” assistant general manager Josh Barfield said. “He’s been facing guys for the first time, and facing really good arms.”</p><p>That includes Milwaukee ace Jacob Misiorowski, who battled to full counts against Murakami on Opening Day only to walk him twice. Later on that game against Brewers reliever Jake Woodford, Murakami hit the first of his three home runs in as many career games, a feat accomplished by only three other big-league rookies upon their debuts.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a90001" name="image-a90001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2fe345a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6338x3557+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1f%2Fba%2Fc649e29c4ef1a09ffe819ccba528%2F2268829800.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/68538a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6338x3557+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1f%2Fba%2Fc649e29c4ef1a09ffe819ccba528%2F2268829800.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Munetaka Murakami takes ground balls before a game against the Brewers last weekend in Milwaukee.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>John Fisher/Getty</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We all knew he had power, but just the ease he's been able to get to the power has really stood out,” Barfield said. “We saw the work that he put in in spring training. You see the confidence that he has, and he's got a long track record of being successful in Japan.”</p><p>Murakami mashed 246 home runs across eight seasons in Nippon Professional Baseball, including 56 in 2022 for the Tokyo Yakult Swallows. Concerns about his strikeout rates drove down his price to the two-year, $34 million deal inked by general manager Chris Getz, and they haven’t evaporated. He’s struck out in nine of his 25 plate appearances.</p><p>“There’s still a long way to go and a lot of ways to improve. So that is what I’ll keep on doing in the upcoming days,” Murakami said via translator in Milwaukee. “Opposing pitchers are mixing it up. It’s really hard to really find out what they’re going to throw… Each and every situation is learning for me so that I can keep growing on that experience.”</p><p>And while some scouts have questioned his defense, Murakami has made a few nice defensive turns at first base after a spring of tutelage under Sox third base coach Justin Jirschele. Murakami mostly played third overseas.</p><p>“He's been great,” said manager Will Venable, who has shuttled Murakami up the batting order from sixth to fourth to second. “He's controlling the zone. He's hitting the ball hard. Defensively, he's done well. He's running extremely hard. He's doing everything we ask him to do.”</p><p>But with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/29/chase-meidroth-white-sox" >few exceptions</a>, Venable hasn’t gotten much else out of his squad in an abysmal first week that has exposed deficiencies in all facets. The Sox have been outscored 52-21, with a team batting average of .192 sitting fifth-worst in baseball and an MLB-high .319 batting average against their pitchers. They’ve also been charged with six fielding errors.</p><p>“Really, that's on me,” Venable said. “Some of these things that we talked about that are important for us, we're just not executing. Whether it's my messaging or whatever, I need to change. We're going to see what we can do to be better at that, starting with me.”</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Brazilian runner who has jogged on nearly every street in Chicago faces departure as student visa expires
entries.summary: <p>After moving to Chicago, Joabe Barbosa made it a priority to immerse himself in his new home.</p><p>But now Barbosa, who has gained attention for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/13/chicago-student-running-chicago" target="_blank" >running through almost every street in Chicago</a> wearing the city's flag as a cape, may not be able to stay.</p><p>Barbosa moved to Chicago in 2022, enrolling at Roosevelt University to pursue a doctorate degree in clinical psychology. He’s on track to finish his degree in 2027, but after he went unmatched for an internship in his final year, the 25-year-old, in the U.S. on an F-1 visa, is at risk of being forced to leave the country and return to his native Brazil.</p><p>“It’s been frustrating,” said Barbosa. “We spend so much money to study and it’s frustrating knowing there aren't a lot of options for international students to stay and finish their degrees.”</p><p>Barbosa, who grew up in London, said his family has taken on debt to help cover the cost of his education, and returning to Brazil would only add to their financial strain. His father has started a <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/help-my-son-continue-his-journey-to-becoming-a-psychologist" target="_blank" ><u>GoFundMe</u></a> to help Barbosa cover the travel cost. The page has raised more than $5,300.</p><p>He said he will have 60 days after the school year ends to leave the country, requiring him to return to Brazil and wait until next year to reapply for an internship and come back to the U.S.</p><p>The internship match process for psychology students can be “incredibly intense for all students,” according to Courtney Kibble, director of the Psychology Program at Roosevelt University.</p><p>There are simply not enough internships for all doctoral candidates, and Kibble said roughly 600 students went unmatched.</p><p>Kimble said the shortage was due to a growing number of doctoral students and limited funding for internship programs. She said despite maintaining a strong GPA, Barbosa’s case was made difficult because a third of the internships are not available to international students.</p><p>“[Joabe] is a phenomenal student in our Doctor of Psychology program,” said Kimble. “For him, it became a numbers game in which the discrepancy of [internship] availability and complication of being an international student impacted him in a negative way. This is in no way a reflection on his preparation or readiness to complete an internship and obtain his doctorate."</p><p>Barbosa took to his <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/reels/DWeUcDqDoAE/" target="_blank" >Instagram</a> account, which has amassed a large following, and got emotional as he announced the news while running through the city. He said his followers have been extremely supportive and some have reached out to try to help him.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; width: 144px;"></div></div></a></div></blockquote> <script async src="//platform.instagram.com/en_US/embeds.js"></script> </div> </div></div><p>However, the likelihood Barbosa will be able to stay in his adopted city is slim.</p><p>“People here have treated me like a true Chicagoan,” said Barbosa. “I don't have a Chicago accent and I haven't lived here for more than five years, but people in Chicago have treated me like I've been here my whole life." </p><p>“I may have to go home, but I’m bringing Chicago with me. I love this city,” he added.</p><p>Barbosa said he wishes there were more opportunities for immigrants in the U.S. and urged other international students in situations similar to his own to stay hopeful.</p><p>“More opportunities will come,” said Barbosa. “It may not seem like it now but they will. I'm going to do things right and I won’t burn any bridges.”</p><p>After suffering a mountain hiking accident in August 2024, Barbosa started running as a way to recover.</p><p>That is what inspired him to become the first person to run through every single street in Chicago. He’s completed just over 85% of Chicago’s map, according to the tracking app City Strides.</p><p>“It’s been amazing,” said Barbosa, who goes on his runs with a Chicago flag tied around him like a cape. “I haven’t been here that long but I’ve loved getting to know each neighborhood and getting to know people.”</p><p>He’s on track to complete the map in a few weeks and is planning a final run June 14 at 10 a.m. He’s inviting all Chicagoans to join him for a run down Michigan Avenue from Oak Street to Ida B Wells Drive.</p><p>Barbosa also holds the Guinness World Record for the fastest known time to visit every CTA station, an endeavor he took on with his friend and brother. They completed it in just under 9 hours.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-410000" entries.title: El regreso a Miami le da a Miguel Vargas una base sólida para destacar en los White Sox
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — El volumen de porras ensordecedoras dentro del cavernoso loanDepot Park esta semana ha desmentido los modestos tamaños de público en Miami e incluso al equipo que recibe esas ovaciones.</p><p>Sólo había 6,515 aficionados reunidos para ver a Miguel Vargas caminar hacia el plato con las bases llenas en la cuarta entrada del lunes. Pero del rugido que siguió a la pelota que despachó en la noche en Florida, podrías haber pensado que Vargas había conectado un jonrón de 402 pies hacia las gradas del jardín izquierdo en Rate Field o ante su afición en su nativa La Habana.</p><p>Sin embargo, aquí son todos juegos de local para Vargas, el residente cubano de Miami que es tan querido por sus <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >compañeros de los White Sox</a> y una oficina que espera que dé un paso significativo en su desarrollo como líder y una pieza clave de su reestructuración organizativa.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-8e0000" name="module-8e0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“No se supone que debas tener favoritos, pero como ser humano, como persona, él es uno de mis favoritos”, expresó el manager Will Venable, quien también adora la versatilidad de Vargas en el campo y en el orden al bate. “Es una persona muy accesible. Se preocupa mucho”.</p><p>Al llegar al lado sur de Chicago en el intercambio por Michael Kopech justo a tiempo para los peores días de la histórica ineptitud de la temporada 2024, los números de Vargas en su primera temporada completa el año pasado fueron sólidos, aunque no necesariamente gritaran “piedra angular de la franquicia”. Bateó .234/.316/.401 con 16 jonrones y 32 dobles en 138 juegos.</p><p>Y el jugador de 26 años ha tenido un buen comienzo en el plato en su segundo año, si el grand slam <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/30/white-sox-vargas-grand-slam-first-win-season" target="_blank" >no lo delata</a>. En cinco juegos, Vargas tiene un promedio de 5 hits en 17 turnos, con un doble y dos boletos. También ha sido constante en la defensa, entrando al año como lo más parecido a un tercera base titular para el incesante reordenador de alineaciones, Venable.</p><p>Vargas consiguió su primer jonrón del año mientras jugaba en primera base, y no tiene pequeñas ambiciones donde quiera que se alinee. Solo pregúntale sobre su <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/22/white-sox-miguel-vargas-working-hard-to-become-complete-third-baseman" target="_blank" >deseo de ganar un Guante de Oro</a>.</p><p>“Definitivamente trato de ser lo mejor que puedo”, dijo Vargas, quien jugó como adolescente para los Industriales de La Habana en la Liga Nacional Cubana.</p><div 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/515820c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4093x2297+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fcb%2F3341bfbe4eab9438b96b7840243c%2Fwhite-sox-marlins-baseball.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Miguel Vargas (izquierda) y Austin Hays celebran tras derrotar a los Miami Marlins el lunes.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Marta Lavandier/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Su padre, Lázaro Vargas, ganó dos medallas de oro olímpicas con Cuba en los años 90, lo que aumentó el interés en la carrera de Miguel en su país natal. Los reporteros de medios en inglés son superados en número esta semana en el vestuario por los medios de comunicación en español que están al tanto de Vargas y otros Sox con lazos locales, como el receptor Edgar Quero y el relevista novato Jedixson Páez, junto con el numeroso grupo de prensa japonesa que sigue a su nuevo amigo y compañero, Munetaka Murakami.</p><p>Vargas tomó bajo su ala a su compañero de primera/tercera base en el entrenamiento de primavera y pidió prestado el guante de primera base de Murakami el lunes.</p><p>“Hemos estado haciendo un muy buen trabajo, especialmente confiando en el tipo que está al lado tuyo, detrás de ti, llegando a base y haciendo las cosas correctas para ganar juegos de béisbol”, dijo Vargas. “Creo que estamos en el lugar correcto en este momento”.</p><p>Después de su noche de seis carreras impulsadas —que lo puso empatado en los primeros lugares de la MLB antes de los juegos del martes— Vargas se reunió con docenas de familiares y amigos en las gradas. “Me han apoyado durante toda mi carrera, y me encanta jugar frente a ellos”, comentó.</p><p>Este momento de su carrera encuentra a Vargas evolucionando hacia un líder del equipo, dijo Venable. “Una vez que te estableces en esta liga, tienes un poco más de tiempo en el deporte, rindes un poco mejor, tu liderazgo vocal tiene la capacidad de crecer un poco más”.</p><p>Otro nuevo compañero de equipo, Austin Hays, dijo que lo reconoció cuando entró al vestuario.</p><p>“Es uno de esos tipos, es como una luz donde quiera que va”, dijo el experimentado jardinero sobre Vargas. “Tienes que tener chicos así en el vestuario. Definitivamente es uno de esos que siempre va a mantener a todos animados y enérgicos”.</p><p>Pero no se trata sólo de carisma.</p><p>“Él tiene mucho poder hacia todos los lados del campo, tiene bastante buena disciplina en el plato”, dijo Hays. “Creo que puede hacer realmente buenos números”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Bears still hope for compensatory picks after meeting with Roger Goodell about Ian Cunningham situation
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> owner George McCaskey believes the organization held true to the spirit of the NFL’s diversity initiative and the letter of it in developing former executive Ian Cunningham, yet the league hasn’t awarded the team two compensatory third-round draft picks as part of the Falcons hiring Cunningham as general manager in January.</p><p>McCaskey, president Kevin Warren and general manager Ryan Poles traveled to New York to present their stance to commissioner Roger Goodell after the league ruled that Falcons president of football Matt Ryan is the team’s “primary football executive,” not Cunningham.</p><p>"We did what the league wants every member club to do,” McCaskey said Wednesday at the Arizona Biltmore. “We identified diverse talent, we recruited him, we created a position for him. ... We allowed him to make mistakes and to learn from those mistakes. We gave him supervisory duties. We gave him training.</p><p>“We made him ready to be a general manager in the NFL, and he's getting his opportunity and we're thrilled for him."</p><p>McCaskey didn’t want to “speculate” on how receptive Goodell was, but believed the Bears “made a pretty compelling case.”</p><p>Cunningham said in February <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/02/24/bears-falcons-agree-that-bears-should-receive-compensatory-3rd-round-draft-picks-for-ian-cunningham-leaving" >his team is in favor of the Bears getting compensatory picks</a>, and Ryan said in an interview with CBS, while emphasizing his own lack of front-office experience, that, “I’ve got a lot to learn ... Ian is in charge and he is driving this boat.”</p><p>McCaskey said there isn’t necessarily opposition within the NFL to the Bears receiving the picks, but the league is weighing the effect of setting a precedent.</p><p>Poles hired Cunningham, who is Black, to the newly formed position of assistant general manager in 2022, and Cunningham has consistently been a general manager candidate. He turned down an offer from the Cardinals in 2023.</p><p>The league announced a finalized draft order three weeks ago, but the Bears remain hopeful that still could be adjusted. If not, their push would be to receive the picks in the 2027 and ’28 drafts.</p><p>McCaskey also brushed aside Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier’s recent attack that the Rooney Rule, which requires teams to interview at least two minority candidates for general manager, head coach and coordinator hirings, is discriminatory and unlawful.</p><p>"The Bears' philosophy is we're committed to diversity, and we will continue our efforts,” McCaskey said. “We're very proud of what we did with Ian, and we're very much looking forward to the next opportunity to do something similar."</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: White Sox jettison Rule 5 rookie Jedixson Paez after three outings
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> designated Rule 5 Draft rookie <b>Jedixson Paez </b>for assignment Wednesday after just a couple of rough outings <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/26/jedixson-paez-white-sox-rookie-roster-rule-5" target="_blank" ><u>to start the 22-year-old reliever’s career</u></a>, while the team picked up the contract of journeyman righty <b>Lucas Sims</b> from Triple-A Charlotte.</p><p>Diamonds like <b>Shane Smith</b> and <b>Mike Vasil</b> don’t always emerge from the Rule 5 rough, but the Sox didn’t exactly ease Paez in for his first three appearances above High-A, where he played last year in the Red Sox organization.</p><p>Manager <b>Will Venable</b> called in Paez for mop-up duty with the bases loaded during their Opening Day <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/26/white-sox-opening-day-loss-brewers-shane-smith-chase-meidroth" >blowout loss</a> to the Brewers. He walked home a run and later served up a three-run homer in a brutal inning and a third.</p><p>Paez had a clean one-pitch, one-out, one-third of an inning in the second game of the Milwaukee set, but he got rocked Tuesday in Miami to put an ugly early line on his MLB stat sheet: four hits, two homers and six runs surrendered over three innings, with three walks and no strikeouts.</p><p>Venable said Paez’s strike-throwing “was really his calling card … and we weren’t able to get there with it.”</p><p>“It’s a tough jump for him,” Venable said. “Just felt like it was the right thing to do, for him and for our group — where we’re going to need those innings, we’re going to need some, truthfully, some better performance out of that roster spot.”</p><p>As a Rule 5 pick, Paez will be offered back to the Red Sox after clearing waivers. Sox pitching coach <b>Zach Bove</b> said, “Obviously it just didn’t work out, so that’s on us.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-portrait-left> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-460000" name="image-460000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium-3x4"> <source type="image/webp" width="375" height="500" alt="Lucas Sims." width="375" height="500" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e56a605/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3744x4992+0+0/resize/375x500!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F23%2F9164c5d545f2881b0f57358f86b0%2Fwhite-sox-2026-baseball.jpg" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Lucas Sims.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Brynn Anderson/AP </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Sims, 31, impressed as a nonroster invitee at spring training for the Sox. A 2012 first-round draft pick on his fifth big-league club, he brought a 4.86 career ERA as a staffer stitched his name and number on a jersey outside the visitors’ clubhouse before Wednesday’s rubber match in Miami.</p><p>Sims struggled last year with the Nationals, giving up 19 runs in 12 ⅓ innings, but said he feels rejuvenated after an offseason focusing on his mental health, “refining my joy and my love for the game.”</p><p>“So many times you can get caught up in the external, out-of-your-control nonsense,” Sims said. “Getting back to just the raw ... kid in the backyard playing the game. It's just not that serious. It was a lot of work, and we're in a brighter spot now.”</p><p>Before recording a strikeout and a walk while hitting a batter and giving up a double in two scoreless innings for his Sox debut, Sims offered some veteran advice for Paez.</p><p>“You just keep going. You just keep pitching. He's got a bright future,” Sims said. “I saw him throw this spring and he has an impressive arm. I don't think by any means that's the last we'll see of him.”</p><h3><b>Hays settling in</b></h3><p>Another veteran addition to the Sox is bullish on their prospects despite a tough start. <b>Austin Hays</b> launched his first home run with the Sox on Monday and made an impressive diving catch Tuesday.</p><p>“Everyone is learning their role and identity on the team,” Hays said. “I’m seeing that grow and build right now, but I’m really excited for when everybody really settles in and gets comfortable with one another. I’m having a lot of fun with this team and this clubhouse so far. It’s going to be a fun year.”</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, carouselAutoPlay=false, promoTitleFont=font-2, ctaButtonText=[], primaryTextColor=#2a2a2a, buttonTextColor=#ffffff, linkTextColor=#1a1a1a, hideTitles=false, footerLinkColor=#ffffff, carouselSpeed=null, buttonHoverBgColor=#a91d23, rteHeadingFont=font-1, secondaryColor=#656565, hideCategories=true, _template=/page/list/includes/PageList-header.hbs, font3=Arial, font4=Source-serif-4, ctaUrl=null, hideImage=false, footerLinkHoverColor=#d3242c, hideAudioPlayback=false, description=[], showSecondItemMedia=false, listColumns234=null, footerTextColor=#ffffff, titleUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox, items=[{type=Image, wordCount=134, authors=[{name=[{items=[Mitchell Armentrout]}], bio=[{items=[Mitchell Armentrout is a staff reporter covering government and politics from Chicago to Springfield, focusing on the expansion of gambling across Illinois as well as the Chicago Bears’ quest for a new stadium. entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Bears chairman George McCaskey fine with Ben Johnson ripping Packers — but knows 'you gotta back it up'
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — Yes, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> chairman George McCaskey was fine with coach Ben Johnson ripping the Packers.</p><p>No, he wasn’t scandalized by Johnson cursing.</p><p>“Ben and I have talked,” he said with a smile Wednesday. “We’re on the same page.”</p><p>That’s the exact same phrasing, with the first name swapped out, that Johnson used when asked what his boss thought of his cursing in the locker room speech he gave his team after their NFC Wild Card win. In a video posted by the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/01/bears-president-kevin-warren-targets-late-spring-or-early-summer-for-stadium-decision" target="_blank" >Bears</a>, Johnson said “F— the Packers. F— them. F—ing hate those guys.”</p><p>McCaskey, who still hands out business cards to Packers fans trying to sell them on crossing over to the blue-and-orange side, is all for Johnson leaning into the rivalry. Especially when the Bears win.</p><p>“It’s been great,” he told the Sun-Times, sitting in an armchair at the Arizona Biltmore the morning after the NFL annual meeting ended. “But you gotta back it up. That was the key. If we don’t beat them in the playoffs, then the narrative is still, ‘Well, you still can’t get past them.’ … The rivalry is 100 plus years old. And, sad to say, the last 30 years they’ve had the better of us.”</p><p></p><p>McCaskey pushed back against the idea that he’s opposed to cursing — one was amplified when the Bears chose to produce a G-rated version of “Hard Knocks” rather than one with dirty words and one without.</p><p>“Ben Johnson is not the first coach in the NFL to drop an F bomb immediately after an emotional game,” he said, “and he’s not going to be the last.”</p><p>Given his role in the founding of the league, George Halas — McCaskey’s grandfather and namesake — might have actually been the first in the NFL. And he, McCaskey said, was a Navy man who enjoyed coarse language.</p><p></p><p>The chairman is generally opposed to cameras filming the raw emotion of a locker room seconds after the game ends, but knows it’s content both national networks and his own social media team wants. He found one benefit to it: the emergence of Johnson’s “Good, Better, Best” cheer. McCaskey said he was touched by parents, coaches and even teachers leading kids in the cheer during the season.</p><p></p><p>“I don’t see how you can take that positive,” he said, “and turn around and say, “Well, I’m offended by what was said right before that.’”</p><p></p><p>In his first public comments since before the Bears’ season began, McCaskey argued that, it some ways, the Bears’ season, filled with harried finishes, was more fun than even their Super Bowl-winning year.</p><p>“In ’85, we were rolling over people,” he said. “This past season we were living on the edge, man. Every game.”</p><p>There were times during the season when McCaskey wondered whether his mother Virginia was intervening. She died last year at 102.</p><p>“Part of it is a testament to the hard work and preparation of the coaches and players,” he said. “At some point you gotta think that somebody was looking out for us.”</p><p>Before her death, Virginia McCaskey gave her heirs access to studies about family businesses — many of them fail in the third and fourth generations. At the NFL’s annual meeting last year, McCaskey talked about the importance of his family sticking together in the wake of her death.</p><p>A year in, McCaskey described the effort as a “collective task” that’s gone well.</p><p>“She wanted us to do everything we could to foresee obstacles and challenges and avoid them,” he said. “But you have to have the commitment that the family is still committed to the business. We certainly have that.”</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Cubs' Seiya Suzuki will continue rehab at Double-A Knoxville
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> outfielder Seiya Suzuki, who has missed the season’s first six games with a sprained right knee, will work out with Double-A Knoxville on Thursday, then begin a rehab assignment with the Smokies when they open their season Friday night.</p><p>Manager Craig Counsell said Suzuki will play the outfield while in Knoxville, and expects him to be gone no more than a week before he rejoins the big-league club.</p><p>“Probably three to seven days, somewhere in that range,’’ Counsell said. “Seeing how he feels while he’s playing, and go from there.’’</p><p>Suzuki has been out since March 14, when he hurt the knee on a stolen base attempt in the first inning of Japan’s quarterfinal loss to Venezuela in the World Baseball Classic in Miami. In his absence, Counsell has used Matt Shaw three times and Michael Conforto twice. Shaw was scheduled to play right field Thursday afternoon.</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Sky trade second-round picks with Portland Fire and Toronto Tempo
entries.summary: <p>Ahead of Friday’s WNBA expansion draft, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> gave up a modest amount of draft capital to ensure the expansion teams would not select any players from their unprotected list.</p><p>The first deal was a second-round swap with the Portland Fire, sending the Sky's No. 17 pick in exchange for Portland’s No. 21 pick. They also sent their No. 26 pick—the final selection in the second round—to the Toronto Tempo.</p><p>In return, the Tempo and Fire agreed not to select any player from the Sky's unprotected list in the expansion draft.</p><p>This deal shows that the Sky are prioritizing existing players—specifically holding onto the rights to their 2025 draft picks, Hailey Van Lith, Maddy Westbeld and Aicha Coulibaly, along with point guard Sevgi Uzun.</p><p>Several of those players would have ended up on the Sky's unprotected list. Teams can protect only five players in the expansion draft, and Angel Reese, Kamilla Cardoso, Ariel Atkins and Ajša Sivka likely would have taken four of those spots.</p><p>The Tempo and Fire were unlikely to select any of the Sky's unrestricted free agents, which include Courtney Vandersloot, Rebecca Allen, Elizabeth Williams, Rachel Banham, Rebecca Allen and Michaela Onyenwere. </p><p>For now, the Sky hold onto their top asset in the upcoming college draft: the No. 5 pick. That could yield college stars such as Kiki Rice (UCLA), Lauren Betts (UCLA) or Flau’jae Johnson, according to recent mock drafts.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Bears owner George McCaskey: Legacy, tradition won't stop potential move to Indiana stadium
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> fans better wrap their minds around the team moving to Indiana. George McCaskey already has.</p><p>Tradition and legacy are incredibly important to McCaskey, the Bears’ chairman and the grandson of George Halas, and the team has been in his family for more than a century. But as stadium plans in Hammond, Indiana, get increasingly serious, he is at peace with being the one who moves the team out of Illinois and assumes the fan base will move past its initial backlash to the idea.</p><p>“I don’t think, in the end, it’s going to matter to people,” he said Wednesday at the end of the NFL annual meeting. “Back in 1976, the New York Football Giants went across state lines to New Jersey. They have been there ever since. The Jets joined them shortly thereafter. And then 35 years later, both teams had an opportunity to reevaluate their situations and recommitted to New Jersey.</p><p>“And somehow, the Republic has survived.”</p><p>That’s a glib response to fans who were irritated when president Kevin Warren went public last December, leading up to a massive game against the Packers, with the idea of building a stadium in Northwest Indiana.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/01/bears-president-kevin-warren-targets-late-spring-or-early-summer-for-stadium-decision" >Bears president Kevin Warren targets late spring or early summer for stadium decision</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/31/nfl-commissioner-roger-goodell-bears-need-stadium-solution-soon-arlington-heights-hammond-indiana-annual-meetings" >NFL commissioner Roger Goodell: Bears need to find stadium solution soon</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/halas-intrigue/2026/04/01/bears-stadium-podcast-nfl-annual-meetings-ian-cunningham-rooney-rule-halas-intrigue" >Halas Intrigue podcast: All quiet on the stadium front</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The Bears have played at Soldier Field since 1971, interrupted only by one season at the University of Illinois during renovations, and were at Wrigley Field for 50 years before that. Nearly their entire history has been in Chicago, which made a potential move to the old Arlington Park racetrack site in Arlington Heights a bit hard to accept for some, let alone venturing outside the state.</p><p>Again, if that happens, the Bears’ message is get over it.</p><p>“Whether we go to Arlington Park or to Hammond, there is going to be an adjustment period,” McCaskey said. “People are allowed some time to get used to it. I think Bears fans are up to it.”</p><p>There was no news on the stadium as McCaskey and Warren talked Wednesday morning. There was insight, such as McCaskey’s comments about potentially moving to Indiana and the tension he expressed about his family having to borrow $2 billion to build, but no actual movement.</p><p>That’s a story in itself. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2021/6/17/22538681/bears-arlington-heights-park-bid-plans-chicago" >June will mark five years since the Bears began this process</a> — Matt Nagy and Justin Fields were on the team — and they still can’t even say in which state they’ll build the stadium.</p><p>Warren didn’t express any frustration over the slow pace. He maintained the Bears, despite the delays, are in “an excellent position” with two very appealing options.</p><p>The Bears own a 326-acre property in Arlington Heights and are exploring a 340-acre purchase in Hammond. They have the legislative approval they need in Indiana, but don’t own property there yet. In Illinois, it’s the reverse as they seek tax certainty from the state legislature, which ends its session May 31.</p><p>It’s no coincidence that date lines up with Warren’s remark Wednesday that the organization wants to make a decision by “late spring, early summer.”</p><p>Anything resembling a deadline, of course, is written in pencil.</p><p>It would seem the main reason to stall now rather than proceed toward Indiana is that the Bears would truly prefer to go to Arlington Heights and are giving that as much time as reasonably possible to materialize.</p><p>Warren repeatedly expressed confidence the Bears would get “shovels in the ground” by the end of last year, which would set a timeline for the stadium opening for the 2028 season, but that didn’t happen. Now they’re looking at the 2029 season, and meanwhile the team will continue playing in a building that commissioner Roger Goodell said is “not at the top of the list” in terms of modern amenities and elevated fan experience.</p><p>Perhaps it was incidental, but as McCaskey discussed the stadium options with a small group of Chicago-based reporters, with Warren sitting in the back of the room, he called Arlington Park “shovel-ready.”</p><p>When the Bears hired Warren in 2023 to replace Ted Phillips, his mission was to get them going the right direction on the field, in business and in their pursuit of a new stadium, but the public perception was primarily that he was brought in to get the stadium deal done after he’d been at the forefront of the Vikings’ stadium project.</p><p>The Bears just had their best season in more than a decade, going 11-6 and winning a playoff game — the business is doing fine, too, with a recent Forbes valuation of $8.2 billion — and Warren told the Sun-Times that gives him a little extra fire to accelerate the stadium.</p><p>“And we will,” he said firmly. “That is the dream, to have a world-class team playing in a world-class stadium.”</p><p>If that happens in Indiana, so be it.</p><p>It certainly wasn’t Plan A and still might not be. But the Bears — and, most importantly, McCaskey — are comfortable with it.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Former Chicago Daily News building could be in line for landmark status
entries.summary: <p>The former Chicago Daily News building, an Art Deco knockout that has eluded landmark status for decades, will be recommended Thursday for the designation — opening the door for public subsidies to help fund a $70 million renovation of the structure.</p><p>The Commission on Chicago Landmarks will hear a request from the Historic Preservation Division of the city's Department of Planning and Development to grant a preliminary designation for the 26-story building, known in recent years as 2 N. Riverside Plaza.</p><p>If approved, the building's most significant features — including its exteriors, riverfront plaza, 200-foot-long second-floor concourse that leads to the Ogilvie Transportation Center pedestrian bridge, and the complex's dazzling Jazz Age first- and second-floor lobby spaces — would be protected.</p><p>The Department of Planning wouldn't comment on the designation. </p><p>The building's new owners, 2 N. Riverside Venture, who acquired the building in June 2025, didn't return a call seeking comment. A major investor in the ownership group is Blue Star Properties, a company with holdings that include <a class="Link" href="https://bluestarproperties.net/locations/the-salt-shed/" target="_blank" >The Salt Shed</a> music venue at 1357 N. Elston Ave.</p><p>The building is among 300 of the city's 17,000 structures built before 1940 that carry a top-rated "red" designation in the Chicago Historic Resources Survey.</p><p>The designation puts the Daily News building in the same architectural company as Frank Lloyd Wright's Robie House in Hyde Park and the champagne bottle-like Carbide & Carbon Building at 230 S. Michigan Ave.</p><p>"It's long overdue," architect Thomas Leslie, author of "Chicago Skyscrapers, 1871-1934," said of the city's move to seek landmark status for the building. "I was actually surprised that it wasn't already landmarked. It's one of these really important buildings of the late '20s, early 1930s — the kind of Modern movement in Chicago."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f70000" name="image-f70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/da32a3a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F15%2F55%2Ff0dc199b4953bee5ef3f37174f57%2Fnews-260402-9.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3c852cf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F15%2F55%2Ff0dc199b4953bee5ef3f37174f57%2Fnews-260402-9.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/107b5dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F15%2F55%2Ff0dc199b4953bee5ef3f37174f57%2Fnews-260402-9.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Pedestrian walks by the revolving door entrances of 2 N. Riverside Plaza " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/107b5dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F15%2F55%2Ff0dc199b4953bee5ef3f37174f57%2Fnews-260402-9.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/679d025/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F15%2F55%2Ff0dc199b4953bee5ef3f37174f57%2Fnews-260402-9.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/107b5dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F15%2F55%2Ff0dc199b4953bee5ef3f37174f57%2Fnews-260402-9.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A pedestrian walks by the Art Deco eastern entrance of the former Chicago Daily News building.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Built in 1929 with seemingly little expense spared, the Chicago Daily News building looked chiseled and forward-looking compared to the neo-Gothic then-headquarters of the Chicago Tribune, which was built in 1925.</p><p>The design was also an architectural expression of the might and influence of the old Chicago Daily News, an important afternoon newspaper with correspondents around the globe.</p><p>It was the first major U.S. structure built on railroad air rights.</p><p>Architects Holabird & Root turned the best and broadest of the slablike block-long building away from Madison and Washington streets and toward the south branch of the Chicago River, making it among the city's first buildings designed to embrace the then-murky and occasionally foul waterway.</p><p>Then, in a masterstroke, the architects designed a wide public riverside plaza as a work of art. An assortment of relief elements on the building's facade enliven the space, including tributes to the early written word and luminaries of the publishing industry.</p><p>"One of my favorite ways to come into the city is to come from the Ogilvie center, over the bridge, through the [Daily News building] corridor and out onto the plaza," Leslie said. "It gives you that great perspective up and down the river. The building was visionary in that sense of providing an urban space, but also understanding that the river was going to become a really important civic element."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f80000" name="image-f80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d2ad1de/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F2f%2F8422360b4cce96cce5cde21479b3%2Fnews-260402-13.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aeced86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F2f%2F8422360b4cce96cce5cde21479b3%2Fnews-260402-13.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/054dab5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F2f%2F8422360b4cce96cce5cde21479b3%2Fnews-260402-13.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Exterior of the former Chicago Daily News building" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/054dab5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F2f%2F8422360b4cce96cce5cde21479b3%2Fnews-260402-13.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0ee191c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F2f%2F8422360b4cce96cce5cde21479b3%2Fnews-260402-13.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/054dab5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F2f%2F8422360b4cce96cce5cde21479b3%2Fnews-260402-13.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Some of the Art Deco architectural details on the former Chicago Daily News building.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>If the city's landmarks commission grants a preliminary designation for the Daily News building on Thursday, the panel will then vote to decide if the structure's planned rehab is eligible for a property tax assessment reclassification that could pour millions into the project.</p><p>The so-called Class L designation has been used to assist in the redevelopment of landmark commercial properties in Cook County.</p><p>The Daily News' planned rehab is expected to cover the building's commercial places, but details beyond that remain sketchy until Thursday's commission meeting.</p><p>The work, if wide enough in scope, is just the thing the building has needed for years. While its interiors look good, the building's patchy and dingy-in-places facade needs help.</p><p>The city didn't seek landmark status for the Daily News building's longtime ownership under the late powerful developer Sam Zell, a choice that placed the building at risk. Zell wanted to raze the property in 2000 and replace it with a new tower, but then-Mayor Richard M. Daley halted the plan.</p><p>In 2008, Zell wanted to destroy the Daily News plaza and squeeze in a new skyscraper between the river and the existing building. Luckily, the recession killed that idea.</p><p>But now under new owners, perhaps the Daily News building can shine once more.</p>
entries.author: Lee Bey

entries.title: West Suburban Medical Center struggled to treat patients months before closing
entries.summary: <p>The owner of West Suburban Medical Center pledged to do everything he could to reopen the safety net hospital by this summer as he defended his leadership in a combative news conference on Wednesday.</p><p>But that comes as hospital inspection reports obtained by WBEZ and the Chicago Sun-Times paint a picture of broader problems at the Oak Park facility, of a hospital struggling to take care of its most critically ill patients — and even keep the heat on — in the months before West Suburban closed.</p><p>During a wide-ranging and chaotic press conference Wednesday, Dr. Manoj Prasad, the hospital's owner and CEO of Resilience Healthcare, said West Suburban was troubled long before he <a class="Link" href="https://www.oakpark.com/2023/01/24/new-ceo-upbeat-about-prospects-for-west-sub/" target="_blank" ><u>bought</u></a> it in 2022.</p><p>“We have already proven ourselves,” Prasad said in a board room packed with reporters at an otherwise empty hospital. “We have come in when nobody would even touch this place. We have kept it up and running for three and a half years.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-340000" name="image-340000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c1bac13/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fee%2F4f2450704f9f8887582e465aa790%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b06e690/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fee%2F4f2450704f9f8887582e465aa790%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-19.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1c550c6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fee%2F4f2450704f9f8887582e465aa790%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Black plastic covers the emergency room signs at West Suburban Medical Center in Oak Park on Wednesday. The hospital’s majority owner announced he was temporarily closing the hospital last week." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1c550c6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fee%2F4f2450704f9f8887582e465aa790%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-19.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e97962c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fee%2F4f2450704f9f8887582e465aa790%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-19.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1c550c6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2Fee%2F4f2450704f9f8887582e465aa790%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-19.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Black plastic covers the emergency room signs at West Suburban Medical Center in Oak Park on Wednesday. The hospital’s majority owner announced he was temporarily closing the hospital last week.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Prasad <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/03/25/west-suburban-medical-center-closing-runs-out-cash-weiss-memorial" target="_blank" ><u>temporarily suspended services at </u></a>West Suburban last week, saying he had no choice because the hospital’s electronic medical record system was not properly billing for services. The hospital took in 10 to 15% of its usual income for the last year, he said.</p><p>But in February and March, the hospital was investigated by the federal government twice for what’s known as “immediate jeopardy,” a serious allegation meaning inspectors believe patients could be at risk of serious harm or death.</p><p>West Suburban couldn’t x-ray three “unstable” patients — the four portable x-ray machines hadn’t worked for days — and didn’t have a doctor on call for several days to treat critically ill patients in the 16-bed intensive care unit, according to an inspection report from the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services.</p><p>A doctor told inspectors it’s difficult to diagnose patients with no portable chest x-ray. “I am guessing,” especially for unstable patients, the doctor said.</p><p>In one case, a patient who had trouble breathing and eventually went into cardiac arrest was resuscitated and brought to the ICU on a ventilator to help them breathe, the inspection report said. Doctors couldn’t get a portable X-ray for the patient’s chest to see if their condition was improving.</p><p>There was only one stationary X-ray machine that worked, but there was no back-up plan if it broke, the inspection report said.</p><p>In another case, a patient in the ICU was in septic shock and had coded. The only physician in the emergency department had to come to the ICU to try to revive the patient, who died.</p><p>An ICU physician who was on vacation during that time said doctors in the emergency department and nurses in the ICU “had no idea who to call if patients needed physician intervention in the ICU,” according to the inspection report.</p><p>Basic services at the hospital were gradually deteriorating, the doctor told inspectors.</p><p>Inspectors also found that the heat was out throughout the hospital in late October as replacement parts for the HVAC system were ordered. In some areas it was just 37 degrees. Some patients said they were comfortable in their rooms, while others were moved to other parts of the hospital, the inspection report said.</p><p>Several of these issues have been addressed, the reports say.</p><p>Patients, lawmakers and community leaders have criticized how abruptly West Suburban <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/03/25/west-suburban-medical-center-closing-runs-out-cash-weiss-memorial" target="_blank" ><u>closed on March 25</u></a>. The <a class="Link" href="https://healthcarereportcard.illinois.gov/hospital/101174" target="_blank" ><u>for-profit</u></a> hospital mostly treats low-income and elderly patients. The majority are Black or Hispanic, <a class="Link" href="https://hfsrb.illinois.gov/content/dam/soi/en/web/hfsrb/documents/inventories-data/facility-profiles/hospital-facility-profiles-(new)/2024-Hospital-Profiles-Individual.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>public health data shows</u></a>.</p><p>Patients were transferred to other hospitals, and employees furloughed. Prasad told the state nearly 750 people worked there.</p><p>The Illinois Department of Public Health has criticized the move, too. A spokesman said it was “a highly unusual and irresponsible situation” for Prasad to notify state regulators the same day he suspended services.</p><p>“The scale and the timing left little time for IDPH to ensure that patient care was protected and that transition were made carefully,” spokesman James Leach wrote in an email. He did not respond to interview requests.</p><p>But the signs were there that West Suburban was unraveling. Prasad had <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health-medicine/2025/08/08/weiss-memorial-hospital-emergency-room-in-uptown-closes" target="_blank" ><u>closed his other hospital</u></a>, Weiss Memorial in Uptown on Chicago’s North Side, last August.</p><p>Prasad <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/ftp/JCAR/AdminCode/077/077011300B02400R.html" target="_blank" ><u>followed the law</u></a> when he suspended services at West Suburban, WBEZ and the Sun-Times confirmed. In fact, he had 30 days to notify the state and local lawmakers after suspending services and explain why. Now he must provide updates to the state every 30 days, said Blanca Dominguez, general counsel for the Illinois Health Facilities and Services Review Board, which regulates the health care industry. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" data-align-center> <a class="AnchorLink" id="gallery-220001" name="gallery-220001"></a> <div> <div class="Carousel-slides"> <div class="Carousel-slide"> <div class="CarouselSlide" > <div class="CarouselSlide-media"> <picture data-crop="large"> <source type="image/webp" width="840" height="555" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d0ec50/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x4045+0+19/resize/840x555!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fd3%2Fcb8a83b64b0b89a28db5a96a3d7a%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/95f4286/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x4045+0+19/resize/1680x1110!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fd3%2Fcb8a83b64b0b89a28db5a96a3d7a%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-02.jpg 2x"/> <source width="840" height="555" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f46a82d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x4045+0+19/resize/840x555!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fd3%2Fcb8a83b64b0b89a28db5a96a3d7a%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-02.jpg"/> <img class="Image" alt="A reporter puts his hands up Wednesday as he asks questions to Resilience Healthcare CEO Manoj Prasad and State Rep. Ford (left) speaks with Resilience Healthcare CEO Manoj Prasad after a press conference Wednesday to discuss the conditions at West Suburban Medical Center in Oak Park." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e432bc4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4083x4083+1020+0/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F21%2Ff776824b4f0386ef3c24681c4dd8%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f6511f1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4083x4083+1020+0/resize/600x600!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F21%2Ff776824b4f0386ef3c24681c4dd8%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05.jpg 2x" width="300" height="300" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e432bc4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4083x4083+1020+0/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F20%2F21%2Ff776824b4f0386ef3c24681c4dd8%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjMwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </span> </div> </div> </div></div><div class="FlickityCustomNavigation-wrapper"> <div class="FlickityCustomNavigation below"> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Rep. Ford pushes for a plan to save West Suburban </b></h3><p>State Rep. La Shawn Ford, whose district includes West Suburban, organized Wednesday's news conference with Prasad. Ford said he was there as a representative for the community.</p><p>“What I want more than anything is for us to have a plan to save this hospital,” Ford said.</p><p>But there has to be some type of infusion of money for West Suburban to reopen, he said.</p><p>“And if we don't get some infusion, I don't know how you're going to open it. You don't know either,” Ford said to Prasad.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d20000" name="image-d20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/29dbe1e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2Fe4%2F023c706a421e84f4b2ab42e7ff08%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dcc8b0f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2Fe4%2F023c706a421e84f4b2ab42e7ff08%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d183c61/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2Fe4%2F023c706a421e84f4b2ab42e7ff08%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="State Rep. La Shawn K. Ford (left) at West Suburban Medical Center with Dr. Manoj Prasad." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d183c61/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2Fe4%2F023c706a421e84f4b2ab42e7ff08%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/95b3ad0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2Fe4%2F023c706a421e84f4b2ab42e7ff08%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d183c61/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F22%2Fe4%2F023c706a421e84f4b2ab42e7ff08%2Fwestsubfolo-260402-05-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>State Rep. La Shawn K. Ford (left) says his role at Wednesday’s press conference with Dr. Manoj Prasad at West Suburban Medical Center was to represent the community. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Ford listed Resilience Healthcare's outstanding debts: $71 million owed to the state of Illinois, $50 million owed to vendors, and unpaid property taxes and mortgage payments.</p><p>Prasad said he and other hospital leaders have a plan to get caught up on about 120,000 outstanding billing claims totaling “hundreds of millions of dollars."</p><p>Prasad hopes once those bills are paid, he'll be able to reopen the hospital by late June or early July.</p><p>“I'm here to serve, and I'm here to stay,” Prasad said.</p><p>Meanwhile, he said roughly three-fourths of the hospital's employees have been furloughed.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"
